name: Alabama

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: The southern tail of an ancient mountain chain that stretches all the way to Canada.
physicalFeatures.fact: These mountains are older than the bones of dinosaurs.

physicalFeatures.name: Tennessee River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that bends through northern Alabama on its way to the Ohio.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries more water than any other river in the Tennessee Valley.

physicalFeatures.name: Mobile Bay
physicalFeatures.description: A large bay on the Gulf Coast where rivers meet the salty sea.
physicalFeatures.fact: Sometimes fish leap onto the beach here during a rare event called a jubilee.

physicalFeatures.name: Gulf of Mexico Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A 60-mile stretch of warm white-sand beaches along the Gulf of Mexico.
physicalFeatures.fact: The sand is made of pure quartz washed down from the Appalachian Mountains.

physicalFeatures.name: Cheaha Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Alabama's tallest mountain, with views that stretch for miles in every direction.
physicalFeatures.fact: It stands 2,413 feet tall — the highest point in the state.

physicalFeatures.name: Alabama River
physicalFeatures.description: A winding river formed where the Coosa and Tallapoosa rivers join together.
physicalFeatures.fact: It curves so much that boats travel twice the straight-line distance to reach the sea.

parks.name: Little River Canyon National Preserve
parks.description: A deep, rocky canyon carved by a river that runs along the top of a mountain.

parks.name: Russell Cave National Monument
parks.description: A huge cave shelter where people lived for almost 10,000 years.

parks.name: Gulf State Park
parks.description: Two miles of white-sand beach with hiking trails through coastal forest.

parks.name: Cheaha State Park
parks.description: Alabama's oldest state park, perched on top of the state's tallest mountain.

parks.name: Horseshoe Bend National Military Park
parks.description: A grassy bend in the river where a major frontier battle was fought in 1814.

parks.name: Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site
parks.description: Honors the first African American military pilots.

parks.name: Oak Mountain State Park
parks.description: Alabama's largest state park with hiking, fishing, and a raptor center.

parks.name: DeSoto State Park
parks.description: Waterfalls and wildflowers atop Lookout Mountain.

parks.name: Lake Guntersville State Park
parks.description: Tennessee River lake park famous for bald eagle sightings.

parks.name: Joe Wheeler State Park
parks.description: Lakeside park with fishing, boating, and a marina.

parks.name: Wind Creek State Park
parks.description: Popular camping park on Lake Martin.

quarter.design: Helen Keller seated reading a book in braille, with her name in English and braille and a longleaf pine branch and magnolias around her.

funFacts: Alabama was the birthplace of the U.S. Space and Rocket Center, where the Saturn V moon rocket was developed in Huntsville.
The state has more navigable inland waterways than any other state in the U.S.
Montgomery was the first capital of the Confederacy before Richmond, Virginia took over.

flag.description: A white field charged with a crimson saltire (diagonal cross), inspired by the Confederate Battle Flag's St. Andrew's cross.

name: Alaska

physicalFeatures.name: Denali
physicalFeatures.description: The tallest mountain in North America, capped in snow year-round.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'the tall one' in the Athabaskan language.

physicalFeatures.name: Yukon River
physicalFeatures.description: A massive river that flows almost 2,000 miles from Canada to the Bering Sea.
physicalFeatures.fact: It freezes solid every winter and people drive trucks across it.

physicalFeatures.name: Alaska Range
physicalFeatures.description: A curving chain of snowy peaks across south-central Alaska.
physicalFeatures.fact: It includes the three tallest mountains in the United States.

physicalFeatures.name: Aleutian Islands
physicalFeatures.description: A long arc of more than 300 islands stretching toward Russia.
physicalFeatures.fact: The chain has over 50 active volcanoes.

physicalFeatures.name: Pacific Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A rugged shoreline of fjords, glaciers, and islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Alaska has more coastline than all other U.S. states combined.

physicalFeatures.name: Mendenhall Glacier
physicalFeatures.description: A 13-mile river of blue ice that ends near Juneau.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its ice is so dense it absorbs every color of light except blue.

parks.name: Denali National Park
parks.description: Six million acres of wild tundra surrounding North America's tallest peak.

parks.name: Glacier Bay National Park
parks.description: A maze of tidewater glaciers, fjords, and snow-capped peaks.

parks.name: Katmai National Park
parks.description: Famous for brown bears that gather to catch leaping salmon.

parks.name: Kenai Fjords National Park
parks.description: Deep ocean inlets surrounded by an enormous ice field.

parks.name: Wrangell-St. Elias National Park
parks.description: The largest national park in the U.S., bigger than Switzerland.

parks.name: Gates of the Arctic National Park
parks.description: A roadless wilderness above the Arctic Circle, untouched by trails.

parks.name: Lake Clark National Park
parks.description: Volcanoes, glaciers, and salmon-filled lakes.

parks.name: Kobuk Valley National Park
parks.description: Remote park with giant sand dunes above the Arctic Circle.

parks.name: Chugach State Park
parks.description: Half-million-acre park right next to Anchorage.

parks.name: Denali State Park
parks.description: Stunning views of Denali, North America's tallest peak.

parks.name: Kachemak Bay State Park
parks.description: Alaska's first state park with glaciers and ocean coves.

quarter.design: A grizzly bear emerging from the water with a salmon in its mouth, with the North Star above.

funFacts: Alaska is the largest state in the U.S., more than twice the size of Texas.
Denali (Mount McKinley) is the tallest mountain in North America at 20,310 feet.
Alaska was purchased from Russia in 1867 for about 2 cents per acre.

flag.description: A dark blue field with eight gold stars forming the Big Dipper and the North Star; designed by 13-year-old Benny Benson.

name: Arizona

physicalFeatures.name: Grand Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: A mile-deep gorge carved by the Colorado River over millions of years.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so wide that you could fit four Empire State Buildings stacked end-to-end inside it.

physicalFeatures.name: Colorado River
physicalFeatures.description: A powerful red-brown river that carved the Grand Canyon and feeds the West.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries enough water to fill 350 million swimming pools each year.

physicalFeatures.name: Sonoran Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A hot desert filled with tall saguaro cactus and surprising bursts of green.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the only place in the world where saguaro cactus grow wild.

physicalFeatures.name: Mogollon Rim
physicalFeatures.description: A 200-mile-long cliff that splits Arizona's high country from its low desert.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name is pronounced 'muggy-own'.

physicalFeatures.name: Humphreys Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Arizona's tallest mountain, a snowcapped volcano near Flagstaff.
physicalFeatures.fact: It rises to 12,633 feet — sometimes there is snow on top in June.

physicalFeatures.name: Petrified Forest
physicalFeatures.description: A colorful field of trees that turned to stone over 200 million years.
physicalFeatures.fact: The 'wood' is now solid quartz crystal in every color of the rainbow.

parks.name: Grand Canyon National Park
parks.description: One of the seven natural wonders of the world, with breathtaking views from the rim.

parks.name: Petrified Forest National Park
parks.description: Ancient trees turned to rainbow-colored stone in a painted desert.

parks.name: Saguaro National Park
parks.description: Two forests of giant saguaro cactus surrounding the city of Tucson.

parks.name: Monument Valley Navajo Tribal Park
parks.description: Towering red sandstone buttes you may recognize from cowboy movies.

parks.name: Canyon de Chelly National Monument
parks.description: Sheer red cliffs hiding 1,000-year-old cliff dwellings.

parks.name: Monument Valley
parks.description: Iconic red sandstone buttes on Navajo tribal land.

parks.name: Wupatki National Monument
parks.description: Red sandstone pueblo ruins from ancient Sinagua people.

parks.name: Sunset Crater Volcano National Monument
parks.description: A 1,000-year-old volcanic cinder cone.

parks.name: Chiricahua National Monument
parks.description: A 'wonderland of rocks' with balanced stone spires.

parks.name: Slide Rock State Park
parks.description: A natural water slide through Oak Creek Canyon's red rocks.

parks.name: Lost Dutchman State Park
parks.description: Named for a legendary lost gold mine in the Superstition Mountains.

parks.name: Kartchner Caverns State Park
parks.description: A 'living' limestone cave with growing formations.

quarter.design: The Grand Canyon with a saguaro cactus in the foreground, separated by a banner that reads 'Grand Canyon State'.

funFacts: The Grand Canyon is up to a mile deep, 18 miles wide, and 277 miles long.
The saguaro cactus, which can grow over 40 feet tall, lives only in the Sonoran Desert.
Arizona has the largest contiguous Ponderosa pine forest in the United States.

flag.description: Thirteen red and gold rays of the setting sun above a blue lower half, with a large copper-colored star at the center honoring Arizona's copper mining heritage.

name: Arkansas

physicalFeatures.name: Ozark Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Rounded, forested mountains full of caves, springs, and clear creeks.
physicalFeatures.fact: They're not really mountains — they're an ancient flat plateau that got carved up.

physicalFeatures.name: Ouachita Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: One of the few mountain ranges in North America that runs east-to-west.
physicalFeatures.fact: They were formed when a continent crashed into North America long ago.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The mighty river forms the entire eastern border of Arkansas.
physicalFeatures.fact: A drop of water takes about three months to float from its start to the sea.

physicalFeatures.name: Arkansas River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that cuts across the state, used by barges carrying grain.
physicalFeatures.fact: It flows 1,469 miles from Colorado all the way to the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Magazine Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Arkansas's highest point, rising above the surrounding forest like a flat-topped table.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 2,753 feet, it's the tallest point between the Appalachians and the Rockies.

physicalFeatures.name: Buffalo River
physicalFeatures.description: A clear, free-flowing river beloved by canoers and floaters.
physicalFeatures.fact: It was the first national river ever designated in the United States.

parks.name: Hot Springs National Park
parks.description: A small city built around 47 natural hot mineral springs.

parks.name: Buffalo National River
parks.description: A wild, clear river that runs free for 135 miles through bluffs and forests.

parks.name: Crater of Diamonds State Park
parks.description: The only place in the world where the public can search for diamonds and keep them.

parks.name: Petit Jean State Park
parks.description: Arkansas's first state park, with waterfalls and stunning bluff views.

parks.name: Pea Ridge National Military Park
parks.description: Rolling fields that hosted a key Civil War battle in 1862.

parks.name: Devil's Den State Park
parks.description: Caves, crevices, and a beautiful CCC-built lake.

parks.name: Mount Magazine State Park
parks.description: Sits on Arkansas's highest mountain with stunning views.

parks.name: Lake Ouachita State Park
parks.description: Crystal-clear lake great for swimming and fishing.

parks.name: Mammoth Spring State Park
parks.description: One of the world's largest springs, flowing 9 million gallons an hour.

parks.name: Queen Wilhelmina State Park
parks.description: Mountain park with a lodge atop Rich Mountain.

parks.name: Cossatot River State Park
parks.description: Wild whitewater river through the Ouachita Mountains.

quarter.design: A diamond, rice stalks, and a flying mallard duck above a lake, representing the state's mining, agriculture, and waterfowl.

funFacts: Arkansas is home to the only active diamond mine in the United States, at Crater of Diamonds State Park, where visitors can keep what they find.
Hot Springs National Park has 47 natural thermal springs flowing at 143 degrees Fahrenheit.
The state has more than 600,000 acres of lakes and 9,700 miles of streams and rivers.

flag.description: A red field with a large white diamond bordered in blue, the diamond representing Arkansas as the only U.S. state where diamonds are mined.

name: California

physicalFeatures.name: Sierra Nevada
physicalFeatures.description: A long, snowy mountain range home to giant sequoia trees and granite cliffs.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'snowy mountains' in Spanish.

physicalFeatures.name: Pacific Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Over 800 miles of cliffs, sandy beaches, and rocky tide pools.
physicalFeatures.fact: Highway 1 hugs this coast and is one of the prettiest drives in the world.

physicalFeatures.name: Mojave Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A high, hot desert dotted with strange Joshua trees and dry lake beds.
physicalFeatures.fact: It includes Death Valley, the hottest place on Earth.

physicalFeatures.name: Central Valley
physicalFeatures.description: A flat, fertile valley that grows much of America's fruits and vegetables.
physicalFeatures.fact: More than a quarter of America's food comes from this single valley.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Tahoe
physicalFeatures.description: A huge, deep mountain lake with water so clear you can see 70 feet down.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the second-deepest lake in the United States.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Whitney
physicalFeatures.description: The tallest mountain in the lower 48 states, with a rocky pyramid summit.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its peak is 14,505 feet — the same height as 14 Empire State Buildings.

physicalFeatures.name: Redwood Forests
physicalFeatures.description: Foggy coastal forests of the tallest trees on Earth.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some redwoods are over 350 feet tall and 2,000 years old.

parks.name: Yosemite National Park
parks.description: Sheer granite cliffs, towering waterfalls, and groves of giant sequoia trees.

parks.name: Sequoia National Park
parks.description: Home to the biggest trees on Earth by total wood volume.

parks.name: Death Valley National Park
parks.description: The hottest and lowest place in North America, with painted hills and sand dunes.

parks.name: Joshua Tree National Park
parks.description: A high desert full of twisted Joshua trees and giant rock piles to climb.

parks.name: Redwood National Park
parks.description: Misty coastal forests of the tallest trees on Earth.

parks.name: Kings Canyon National Park
parks.description: A glacier-carved canyon deeper than the Grand Canyon in places.

parks.name: Lassen Volcanic National Park
parks.description: Boiling mud pots, steaming vents, and an active volcano.

parks.name: Channel Islands National Park
parks.description: Five islands often called the 'Galapagos of North America'.

parks.name: Pinnacles National Park
parks.description: Ancient volcanic rock spires and home to endangered California condors.

parks.name: Point Reyes National Seashore
parks.description: Wild Pacific coastline with elephant seals and a historic lighthouse.

parks.name: Muir Woods National Monument
parks.description: Ancient coast redwoods just north of San Francisco.

quarter.design: John Muir admiring Yosemite Valley's Half Dome, with a California condor soaring overhead.

funFacts: California has the tallest tree (a redwood named Hyperion), the largest tree by volume (General Sherman, a giant sequoia), and the oldest tree (a bristlecone pine over 4,800 years old).
Death Valley is the hottest, driest, and lowest place in North America at 282 feet below sea level.
If California were a country, its economy would be one of the largest in the world.

flag.description: A white field with a grizzly bear walking on green grass above a red stripe, with a red star in the upper left and the words 'California Republic' below the bear.

name: Colorado

physicalFeatures.name: Rocky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: The continent's spine of jagged, snow-capped peaks runs right through Colorado.
physicalFeatures.fact: Colorado has 58 mountains taller than 14,000 feet — more than any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Colorado River
physicalFeatures.description: A powerful river that starts as snowmelt high in the Rockies.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its headwaters are inside Rocky Mountain National Park.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Flat grasslands that cover the eastern half of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: From the plains, you can see mountains rise like a wall 100 miles away.

physicalFeatures.name: Royal Gorge
physicalFeatures.description: A narrow canyon with sheer 1,250-foot walls carved by the Arkansas River.
physicalFeatures.fact: A bridge over the gorge was once the highest in the world.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Elbert
physicalFeatures.description: The tallest mountain in the Rockies, an easy climb compared to its neighbors.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 14,440 feet, it's the second-tallest peak in the lower 48 states.

physicalFeatures.name: Sand Dunes
physicalFeatures.description: North America's tallest sand dunes, piled at the foot of snowy mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some dunes are 750 feet tall — and you can sled down them.

parks.name: Rocky Mountain National Park
parks.description: Towering peaks, alpine lakes, and herds of elk in a high-mountain wonderland.

parks.name: Mesa Verde National Park
parks.description: Ancient stone cliff dwellings built into the sides of canyon walls.

parks.name: Great Sand Dunes National Park
parks.description: North America's tallest sand dunes set against snowcapped mountains.

parks.name: Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park
parks.description: A canyon so deep and narrow that sunlight barely reaches the bottom.

parks.name: Dinosaur National Monument
parks.description: A cliff face full of real dinosaur bones still embedded in the rock.

parks.name: Garden of the Gods
parks.description: Towering red sandstone fins and balanced rocks free to explore.

parks.name: Colorado National Monument
parks.description: Red rock canyons and towering monoliths.

parks.name: Florissant Fossil Beds National Monument
parks.description: Ancient petrified redwood stumps and fossil insects.

parks.name: Eldorado Canyon State Park
parks.description: Famous rock-climbing canyon with sandstone cliffs.

parks.name: Roxborough State Park
parks.description: Dramatic red rock formations south of Denver.

quarter.design: A sweeping view of the Rocky Mountains with evergreen trees and a banner reading 'Colorful Colorado'.

funFacts: Colorado has the highest average elevation of any state, with more than 50 mountains over 14,000 feet tall.
Denver is called the Mile-High City because it sits exactly 5,280 feet above sea level.
The world's largest flat-top mountain, Grand Mesa, is in Colorado.

flag.description: Three horizontal stripes of blue, white, and blue, with a large red letter 'C' encircling a golden disk representing Colorado's sunshine and gold.

name: Connecticut

physicalFeatures.name: Connecticut River
physicalFeatures.description: New England's longest river splits the state down the middle on its way to the sea.
physicalFeatures.fact: It flows 410 miles from the Canadian border to Long Island Sound.

physicalFeatures.name: Long Island Sound
physicalFeatures.description: A long, salty arm of the Atlantic that hugs Connecticut's southern shore.
physicalFeatures.fact: Despite being called a sound, it's home to oysters, seals, and even whales.

physicalFeatures.name: Berkshire Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Rolling, forested hills in the northwest corner that turn brilliant colors in fall.
physicalFeatures.fact: They're the southern end of Vermont's Green Mountains.

physicalFeatures.name: Housatonic River
physicalFeatures.description: A meandering river known for covered bridges and quiet fishing spots.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name comes from a Native American word meaning 'beyond the mountain place.'

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Frissell
physicalFeatures.description: The slope of this mountain is Connecticut's highest point — but the summit is in Massachusetts.
physicalFeatures.fact: The highest spot in Connecticut isn't even on top of a mountain.

physicalFeatures.name: Thimble Islands
physicalFeatures.description: A scattered group of about 100 small rocky islands just off the coast.
physicalFeatures.fact: Many are tiny — some hold only a single house.

parks.name: Weir Farm National Historical Park
parks.description: An old farm where an American painter and his friends made art outdoors.

parks.name: Hammonasset Beach State Park
parks.description: Connecticut's longest public beach, perfect for swimming and shell hunting.

parks.name: Sleeping Giant State Park
parks.description: A line of hills that, from a distance, looks like a giant lying on its back.

parks.name: Dinosaur State Park
parks.description: Real fossilized dinosaur footprints preserved under a big dome.

parks.name: Gillette Castle State Park
parks.description: A stone castle built by an actor on a hilltop above the Connecticut River.

parks.name: Talcott Mountain State Park
parks.description: Heublein Tower offers views of four states.

parks.name: Kent Falls State Park
parks.description: Series of waterfalls cascading down 250 feet.

parks.name: Devil's Hopyard State Park
parks.description: Chapman Falls and unusual rock potholes.

parks.name: Macedonia Brook State Park
parks.description: Mountain views and hiking in the northwest hills.

parks.name: Rocky Neck State Park
parks.description: Sandy beach and salt marsh on Long Island Sound.

quarter.design: The Charter Oak, a famous tree where Connecticut's royal charter was hidden in 1687 to protect it from being taken away by the king.

funFacts: The first hamburger and the first lollipop are both said to have been made in Connecticut.
Connecticut is home to the oldest U.S. newspaper still being published, the Hartford Courant, founded in 1764.
The Mark Twain House in Hartford is where the author wrote some of his most famous books, including The Adventures of Tom Sawyer.

flag.description: A blue field with a white shield bearing three grapevines and the state motto 'Qui Transtulit Sustinet' (He who transplanted still sustains) on a banner below.

name: Delaware

physicalFeatures.name: Delaware Bay
physicalFeatures.description: A huge, shallow bay where the Delaware River meets the Atlantic Ocean.
physicalFeatures.fact: Hundreds of thousands of horseshoe crabs lay eggs on its beaches each spring.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A 28-mile stretch of white-sand beaches popular with summer vacationers.
physicalFeatures.fact: Delaware's coast is so flat that the entire state's highest point is just 448 feet.

physicalFeatures.name: Delaware River
physicalFeatures.description: A historic river that forms much of Delaware's western boundary.
physicalFeatures.fact: George Washington famously crossed this river in a daring Christmas attack in 1776.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Cypress Swamp
physicalFeatures.description: A dark, mysterious swamp of bald cypress trees with knobby knees poking from the water.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the northernmost cypress swamp in the United States.

physicalFeatures.name: Ebright Azimuth
physicalFeatures.description: Delaware's highest point is just a small rise next to a suburban road.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 448 feet, it's the lowest 'highest point' of any U.S. state.

physicalFeatures.name: Brandywine Creek
physicalFeatures.description: A small, swift creek that powered America's first paper and gunpowder mills.
physicalFeatures.fact: Artists have painted its rolling banks for over 200 years.

parks.name: First State National Historical Park
parks.description: Sites that tell the story of America's first state to ratify the Constitution.

parks.name: Cape Henlopen State Park
parks.description: A windswept point of dunes and beach where the bay meets the ocean.

parks.name: Bombay Hook National Wildlife Refuge
parks.description: A vast tidal marsh where shorebirds gather by the thousands.

parks.name: Trap Pond State Park
parks.description: A bald cypress swamp you can paddle a canoe through.

parks.name: Brandywine Creek State Park
parks.description: Rolling meadows and stone walls along a swift, scenic creek.

parks.name: Delaware Seashore State Park
parks.description: Six miles of ocean beach perfect for swimming and surfing.

parks.name: White Clay Creek State Park
parks.description: Forests and streams great for hiking and biking.

quarter.design: Caesar Rodney on horseback, racing 80 miles to Philadelphia to cast the deciding vote for American independence on July 2, 1776.

funFacts: Delaware was the first state to ratify the U.S. Constitution on December 7, 1787.
It is the second-smallest state in the U.S., only larger than Rhode Island.
Delaware has no sales tax, making it a popular place for shopping trips.

flag.description: A colonial-blue field with a buff-colored diamond containing the state coat of arms, and the date of Delaware's ratification of the Constitution—December 7, 1787—below.

name: District of Columbia

physicalFeatures.name: Potomac River
physicalFeatures.description: The broad river that flows past the U.S. Capitol and the Washington Monument.
physicalFeatures.fact: George Washington picked the spot for the capital city beside this river.

physicalFeatures.name: Anacostia River
physicalFeatures.description: A smaller river that joins the Potomac in the heart of the city.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name comes from a Native American village that once stood on its banks.

physicalFeatures.name: Rock Creek
physicalFeatures.description: A wild creek tumbling through a forested park in the middle of the city.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its valley has been protected as parkland since 1890.

physicalFeatures.name: Tidal Basin
physicalFeatures.description: A small inlet ringed by famous Japanese cherry trees that bloom in spring.
physicalFeatures.fact: Japan gave Washington 3,000 cherry trees as a gift of friendship in 1912.

parks.name: National Mall
parks.description: A long, grassy park lined with monuments, memorials, and museums.

parks.name: Rock Creek Park
parks.description: One of the country's biggest city parks, full of forests and hiking trails.

parks.name: Constitution Gardens
parks.description: A peaceful pond and lawn honoring the people who signed the Constitution.

parks.name: President's Park (White House)
parks.description: The lawns and gardens that surround the home of the U.S. President.

parks.name: Ford's Theatre National Historic Site
parks.description: The theater where President Lincoln was shot, now a working stage and museum.

parks.name: Theodore Roosevelt Island
parks.description: A wooded island memorial to America's conservationist president.

parks.name: Anacostia Park
parks.description: Riverside park with sports fields and a roller-skating pavilion.

funFacts: Washington, D.C. was designed by French-born architect Pierre Charles L'Enfant in 1791.
The District is home to the White House, the U.S. Capitol, and the Supreme Court — the three branches of the U.S. government.
No buildings in D.C. can be taller than the Capitol dome, which is why the city has no skyscrapers.

flag.description: A white field with three red stars above two horizontal red bars, based on the coat of arms of George Washington's family.

name: Florida

physicalFeatures.name: Everglades
physicalFeatures.description: A slow, grassy river the size of a small state, full of alligators and wading birds.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the only place on Earth where alligators and crocodiles live together.

physicalFeatures.name: Florida Keys
physicalFeatures.description: A 120-mile string of small tropical islands connected by bridges.
physicalFeatures.fact: One bridge in the chain is over 7 miles long.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Long sandy beaches and warm currents on Florida's eastern shore.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Gulf Stream flows just offshore and is warm enough to swim in year-round.

physicalFeatures.name: Gulf of Mexico Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Calm, warm waters and sugar-white sand on Florida's western shore.
physicalFeatures.fact: The sand here is so fine it squeaks when you walk on it.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Okeechobee
physicalFeatures.description: A huge but very shallow freshwater lake — the largest lake in Florida.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so big you can't see across it, but it averages only 9 feet deep.

physicalFeatures.name: St. Johns River
physicalFeatures.description: One of the few rivers in North America that flows north instead of south.
physicalFeatures.fact: It drops only one inch per mile — almost no slope at all.

parks.name: Everglades National Park
parks.description: A vast wetland of sawgrass, mangroves, and alligators.

parks.name: Biscayne National Park
parks.description: A park that is 95% water, with coral reefs, mangroves, and tiny islands.

parks.name: Dry Tortugas National Park
parks.description: A remote island fort 70 miles from Key West, reached only by boat or plane.

parks.name: Wekiwa Springs State Park
parks.description: Crystal-clear springs that stay 72 degrees all year, perfect for swimming.

parks.name: Canaveral National Seashore
parks.description: An unspoiled 24-mile beach right next to NASA's rocket launch sites.

parks.name: Castillo de San Marcos National Monument
parks.description: A 300-year-old Spanish stone fort in St. Augustine.

parks.name: Big Cypress National Preserve
parks.description: Swampy wilderness next to the Everglades, home to Florida panthers.

parks.name: Bahia Honda State Park
parks.description: One of the most beautiful beaches in the Florida Keys.

parks.name: Anastasia State Park
parks.description: Four miles of beach and ancient sand dunes.

parks.name: Myakka River State Park
parks.description: One of Florida's oldest state parks with alligators and bird watching.

parks.name: Ichetucknee Springs State Park
parks.description: Famous for tubing down a crystal-clear spring-fed river.

parks.name: Honeymoon Island State Park
parks.description: Florida's most popular state park with sandy beaches and ospreys.

quarter.design: A 16th-century Spanish galleon, a space shuttle, and a strip of land with sabal palm trees, with the words 'Gateway to Discovery'.

funFacts: Florida has the longest coastline in the contiguous 48 states, with about 1,350 miles.
St. Augustine, founded in 1565, is the oldest continuously inhabited European-founded city in the U.S.
The Everglades is the only place in the world where alligators and crocodiles live side by side.

flag.description: A white field with a red saltire (diagonal cross) and the state seal at the center, depicting a Seminole woman, a sabal palm, and a steamboat.

name: Georgia

physicalFeatures.name: Blue Ridge Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Misty blue mountains in northern Georgia, the southern tip of the Appalachians.
physicalFeatures.fact: They look blue from a distance because the trees release a fine blue haze.

physicalFeatures.name: Chattahoochee River
physicalFeatures.description: A river that begins in the Blue Ridge Mountains and flows past Atlanta.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'painted rocks' in the Muscogee language.

physicalFeatures.name: Okefenokee Swamp
physicalFeatures.description: A huge dark-water swamp full of cypress trees, alligators, and floating islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'land of trembling earth' because the peat floors actually shake.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A 100-mile coast of barrier islands, salt marshes, and tidal creeks.
physicalFeatures.fact: Georgia has more salt marsh than any other state on the East Coast.

physicalFeatures.name: Brasstown Bald
physicalFeatures.description: Georgia's highest mountain, with a 360-degree view from the summit tower.
physicalFeatures.fact: On a clear day, you can see four states from the top.

physicalFeatures.name: Savannah River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that forms the entire border between Georgia and South Carolina.
physicalFeatures.fact: Big ocean ships travel up it to the historic port of Savannah.

parks.name: Cumberland Island National Seashore
parks.description: A wild barrier island where horses roam free on the beach.

parks.name: Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area
parks.description: A 48-mile stretch of river protected for fishing, hiking, and rafting.

parks.name: Stone Mountain Park
parks.description: A massive dome of solid granite that rises 825 feet above the ground.

parks.name: Providence Canyon State Park
parks.description: Sometimes called 'Georgia's Little Grand Canyon' — a colorful canyon made by erosion.

parks.name: Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park
parks.description: Ancient earth pyramids built by Native peoples more than 1,000 years ago.

parks.name: Fort Pulaski National Monument
parks.description: A massive brick fort guarding the Savannah River.

parks.name: Andersonville National Historic Site
parks.description: A solemn site that was a Confederate prison camp.

parks.name: Cloudland Canyon State Park
parks.description: Deep canyon and waterfalls atop Lookout Mountain.

parks.name: Amicalola Falls State Park
parks.description: Home to one of the tallest waterfalls in the eastern U.S.

parks.name: Tallulah Gorge State Park
parks.description: A two-mile-long, 1,000-foot-deep gorge.

quarter.design: A peach, the state's nickname symbol, framed by sprigs of live oak and a banner reading 'Wisdom, Justice, Moderation'.

funFacts: Georgia is the largest state east of the Mississippi River by land area.
Coca-Cola was invented in Atlanta, Georgia in 1886 by pharmacist John Pemberton.
The Okefenokee Swamp is one of the largest freshwater wetlands in North America.

flag.description: Three horizontal stripes of red, white, and red, with a blue square in the upper left containing the state coat of arms encircled by 13 white stars.

name: Hawaii

physicalFeatures.name: Mauna Kea
physicalFeatures.description: A dormant volcano so tall its peak is often dusted with snow.
physicalFeatures.fact: Measured from the seafloor, it's the tallest mountain on Earth — taller than Everest.

physicalFeatures.name: Kilauea
physicalFeatures.description: One of the most active volcanoes in the world, often erupting bright red lava.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has been erupting almost continuously for decades.

physicalFeatures.name: Hawaiian Islands
physicalFeatures.description: A chain of 8 main islands formed by underwater volcanoes.
physicalFeatures.fact: Hawaii is the only U.S. state made entirely of islands.

physicalFeatures.name: Waimea Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: A deep red canyon often called 'the Grand Canyon of the Pacific.'
physicalFeatures.fact: It's 10 miles long and over 3,000 feet deep.

physicalFeatures.name: Pacific Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Hundreds of miles of black, green, white, and golden beaches.
physicalFeatures.fact: Hawaii has beaches with sand of four different colors.

physicalFeatures.name: Na Pali Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Towering green cliffs that drop straight into the sea on Kauai.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some cliffs are over 3,000 feet high — taller than the world's tallest building.

parks.name: Hawaii Volcanoes National Park
parks.description: Active volcanoes where you can see new land being created.

parks.name: Haleakala National Park
parks.description: A massive dormant volcano so wide you could fit Manhattan inside its crater.

parks.name: Waimea Canyon State Park
parks.description: Hawaii's 'Grand Canyon,' carved deep into a volcano.

parks.name: Diamond Head State Monument
parks.description: An iconic volcanic crater on the edge of Honolulu with a hike to the rim.

parks.name: Pu'uhonua o Honaunau National Historical Park
parks.description: A sacred place of refuge from ancient Hawaiian times.

parks.name: Pearl Harbor National Memorial
parks.description: Honors those lost in the December 7, 1941 attack.

parks.name: Puuhonua o Honaunau National Historical Park
parks.description: An ancient Hawaiian 'place of refuge' with carved wooden statues.

parks.name: Kaloko-Honokohau National Historical Park
parks.description: Preserves ancient Hawaiian fishponds and petroglyphs.

parks.name: Napali Coast State Wilderness Park
parks.description: Towering green sea cliffs along Kauai's wild coast.

parks.name: Akaka Falls State Park
parks.description: A 442-foot waterfall on the Big Island.

parks.name: Iolani Palace
parks.description: The only royal palace on U.S. soil.

parks.name: Polihale State Park
parks.description: Remote western Kauai beach with massive sand dunes.

motto: Ua mau ke ea o ka aina i ka pono

quarter.design: King Kamehameha I stretching his hand toward the Hawaiian Islands, with the motto 'Ua Mau Ke Ea O Ka Aina I Ka Pono'.

funFacts: Hawaii is the only U.S. state made up entirely of islands — 137 in total, though only 7 are inhabited.
Mauna Kea, measured from its base on the ocean floor, is the tallest mountain in the world at over 33,000 feet.
Hawaii is the only state that grows coffee commercially.

flag.description: Eight horizontal stripes of white, red, and blue representing Hawaii's eight main islands, with a Union Jack in the upper-left corner reflecting historical ties to Britain.

name: Idaho

physicalFeatures.name: Rocky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Snow-capped peaks cover much of Idaho's central and northern regions.
physicalFeatures.fact: Idaho has more wilderness land than any state outside Alaska.

physicalFeatures.name: Snake River
physicalFeatures.description: A great curving river that loops through southern Idaho for over 1,000 miles.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carved Hells Canyon — North America's deepest river gorge.

physicalFeatures.name: Hells Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: The deepest river-cut gorge in North America, even deeper than the Grand Canyon.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's almost 8,000 feet deep — over a mile and a half down.

physicalFeatures.name: Sawtooth Range
physicalFeatures.description: A row of jagged granite peaks that really do look like the teeth of a saw.
physicalFeatures.fact: The range has more than 50 peaks over 10,000 feet tall.

physicalFeatures.name: Borah Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Idaho's tallest mountain, a steep climb up loose rock to a sharp summit.
physicalFeatures.fact: It grew taller during a 1983 earthquake.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Pend Oreille
physicalFeatures.description: Idaho's largest lake, deep and cold in the northern panhandle.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so deep the Navy uses it to test submarines.

parks.name: Yellowstone National Park
parks.description: A small slice of America's first national park reaches into Idaho.

parks.name: Craters of the Moon National Monument
parks.description: A black volcanic landscape so alien NASA trained astronauts here.

parks.name: City of Rocks National Reserve
parks.description: Strange granite spires that pioneers carved their names on along the trail.

parks.name: Bruneau Dunes State Park
parks.description: Home to the tallest single-structured sand dune in North America.

parks.name: Hells Canyon National Recreation Area
parks.description: Protected wilderness around North America's deepest river canyon.

parks.name: Hagerman Fossil Beds National Monument
parks.description: World-famous site for ancient horse fossils.

parks.name: Minidoka National Historic Site
parks.description: Honors Japanese Americans imprisoned here during WWII.

parks.name: Ponderosa State Park
parks.description: Forested peninsula jutting into Payette Lake.

parks.name: Heyburn State Park
parks.description: The oldest state park in the Pacific Northwest.

parks.name: Farragut State Park
parks.description: Once a WWII naval training base, now a lakeside playground.

parks.name: Harriman State Park
parks.description: Famous for trumpeter swans and a historic cattle ranch.

parks.name: Thousand Springs State Park
parks.description: Springs gushing from canyon walls along the Snake River.

quarter.design: A peregrine falcon with the outline of Idaho behind it and a banner reading 'Esto Perpetua' meaning 'Let it be perpetual'.

funFacts: Idaho grows about one-third of all the potatoes eaten in the United States.
Hells Canyon, on the Idaho-Oregon border, is the deepest river gorge in North America — deeper than the Grand Canyon.
Nearly every type of gemstone has been found in Idaho, including the rare star garnet, found in only one other place in the world.

flag.description: A blue field with the state seal—the only U.S. state seal designed by a woman, Emma Edwards Green—surrounded by gold fringe and a red banner reading 'State of Idaho'.

name: Illinois

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The mighty Mississippi forms Illinois's entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Steamboats once carried passengers and goods all along Illinois's river edge.

physicalFeatures.name: Illinois River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that flows from Chicago southwest to the Mississippi.
physicalFeatures.fact: A canal connects it to Lake Michigan, linking the Great Lakes to the sea.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Michigan
physicalFeatures.description: One of the five Great Lakes, with a sandy beach right next to downtown Chicago.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the only one of the Great Lakes that lies entirely within the United States.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Flat, fertile farmland covers most of Illinois — perfect for growing corn.
physicalFeatures.fact: Illinois grows more pumpkins than any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Shawnee Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Forested hills and strange rock formations in the southern tip of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: Glaciers stopped right at this line — leaving the hills untouched.

physicalFeatures.name: Charles Mound
physicalFeatures.description: Illinois's highest point is a quiet hill on private farmland.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,235 feet, it's open to visitors only a few days each year.

parks.name: Indiana Dunes National Park
parks.description: Tall sand dunes on the southern shore of Lake Michigan, mostly in Indiana.

parks.name: Starved Rock State Park
parks.description: Sandstone canyons with seasonal waterfalls along the Illinois River.

parks.name: Shawnee National Forest
parks.description: Illinois's only national forest, with wild canyons and rock formations.

parks.name: Garden of the Gods (Illinois)
parks.description: Strange wind-carved sandstone formations on a forested ridge.

parks.name: Lincoln Home National Historic Site
parks.description: The only home Abraham Lincoln ever owned, preserved in Springfield.

parks.name: Pullman National Historical Park
parks.description: A planned 1880s factory town and birthplace of the labor movement.

parks.name: Matthiessen State Park
parks.description: Wooded canyon park with seasonal waterfalls.

parks.name: Garden of the Gods
parks.description: Strange rock formations in the Shawnee National Forest.

parks.name: Giant City State Park
parks.description: Sandstone bluffs that look like a giant city's streets.

parks.name: Cahokia Mounds State Historic Site
parks.description: Ancient earthwork mounds of a once-huge Native American city.

parks.name: Chain O'Lakes State Park
parks.description: Park connecting three lakes great for boating and fishing.

parks.name: Mississippi Palisades State Park
parks.description: High limestone bluffs along the Mississippi River.

parks.name: Pere Marquette State Park
parks.description: Illinois's largest state park, great for fall colors.

quarter.design: A young Abraham Lincoln in front of the state outline, the Chicago skyline, and a farm scene with 21 stars (Illinois was the 21st state).

funFacts: Abraham Lincoln spent most of his life in Illinois, which is why the state is also called 'The Land of Lincoln'.
The first skyscraper in the world was built in Chicago in 1885 — the 10-story Home Insurance Building.
Illinois has more than 1,000 miles of bike trails and is home to the famous Route 66 highway.

flag.description: A white field featuring the state seal: an eagle holding a banner reading 'State Sovereignty, National Union' perched on a boulder above a rising sun.

name: Indiana

physicalFeatures.name: Wabash River
physicalFeatures.description: Indiana's state river, immortalized in the state song.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the longest free-flowing river east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Michigan
physicalFeatures.description: Indiana has a 45-mile slice of this Great Lake on its northwest corner.
physicalFeatures.fact: Shipping on the lake helped build Chicago and northwest Indiana into industrial centers.

physicalFeatures.name: Ohio River
physicalFeatures.description: A broad river that forms Indiana's entire southern border with Kentucky.
physicalFeatures.fact: It once carried boats full of pioneers headed west into Indiana.

physicalFeatures.name: Indiana Dunes
physicalFeatures.description: Towering sand dunes along Lake Michigan, some over 100 feet tall.
physicalFeatures.fact: The tallest dune, Mount Tom, stands 192 feet above the lake.

physicalFeatures.name: Hoosier Hill
physicalFeatures.description: Indiana's highest point — just a small rise in a soybean field.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,257 feet, it's one of the easiest 'highest points' to reach.

physicalFeatures.name: Hoosier National Forest
physicalFeatures.description: Indiana's only national forest, covering hills, caves, and hollows in the south.
physicalFeatures.fact: It covers 204,000 acres of rolling hardwood forest.

parks.name: Indiana Dunes National Park
parks.description: Towering sand dunes and beaches along the southern shore of Lake Michigan.

parks.name: Brown County State Park
parks.description: Indiana's largest state park, famous for fall colors and forested hills.

parks.name: Turkey Run State Park
parks.description: Deep sandstone canyons with ladders and bridges for adventuring.

parks.name: Hoosier National Forest
parks.description: Indiana's only national forest, full of hidden caves and creeks.

parks.name: Lincoln Boyhood National Memorial
parks.description: The Indiana farm where young Abraham Lincoln grew up.

parks.name: George Rogers Clark National Historical Park
parks.description: Honors a key Revolutionary War victory in the West.

parks.name: Indiana Dunes State Park
parks.description: Tall sand dunes you can hike up for lake views.

parks.name: Spring Mill State Park
parks.description: A restored pioneer village with a working grist mill.

parks.name: McCormick's Creek State Park
parks.description: Indiana's first state park, with a beautiful waterfall.

parks.name: Clifty Falls State Park
parks.description: Series of waterfalls and a deep, rugged canyon.

parks.name: Pokagon State Park
parks.description: Famous for its quarter-mile-long refrigerated toboggan run.

quarter.design: A racecar overlaid on the state outline with 19 stars (Indiana was the 19th state) and a banner reading 'Crossroads of America'.

funFacts: The Indianapolis 500, one of the most famous car races in the world, has been held in Indiana every year since 1911.
Wabash, Indiana was the first city in the world to be lit entirely by electric streetlights, in 1880.
Indiana is one of the leading producers of popcorn in the U.S.

flag.description: A blue field with a gold torch surrounded by 19 stars representing Indiana as the 19th state to join the Union, designed by Paul Hadley for the state's centennial.

name: Iowa

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The Mississippi forms Iowa's entire eastern border, full of barges and bluffs.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some of America's tallest river bluffs rise right out of Iowa's side of the river.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri forms Iowa's western border on its way south to the Mississippi.
physicalFeatures.fact: Lewis and Clark camped along Iowa's banks on their famous expedition.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Rolling farmland covers nearly all of Iowa — the most farmed state in America.
physicalFeatures.fact: 85% of Iowa's land is used for growing crops.

physicalFeatures.name: Loess Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Rolling hills made entirely of fine, wind-blown dust from the Ice Age.
physicalFeatures.fact: Hills made of loess this large only exist in two places — Iowa and China.

physicalFeatures.name: Hawkeye Point
physicalFeatures.description: Iowa's highest point is just a gentle rise on a cattle farm.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,670 feet, it's marked by a giant pole topped with a hawk.

physicalFeatures.name: Des Moines River
physicalFeatures.description: Iowa's longest interior river, flowing across the state to the Mississippi.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name comes from a French word meaning 'of the monks.'

parks.name: Effigy Mounds National Monument
parks.description: Ancient earth mounds shaped like bears and birds, built by Native peoples.

parks.name: Herbert Hoover National Historic Site
parks.description: The tiny cottage where the 31st U.S. President was born.

parks.name: Maquoketa Caves State Park
parks.description: More than a dozen caves you can explore with a flashlight.

parks.name: Backbone State Park
parks.description: Iowa's oldest state park, with limestone bluffs and a clear winding river.

parks.name: Ledges State Park
parks.description: Towering sandstone bluffs along a wandering creek.

parks.name: Pikes Peak State Park
parks.description: High bluff overlooking the Mississippi River.

parks.name: Wildcat Den State Park
parks.description: Sandstone formations and a historic grist mill.

parks.name: Lake Macbride State Park
parks.description: Iowa's largest state-owned lake, great for boating.

parks.name: Palisades-Kepler State Park
parks.description: Limestone cliffs along the Cedar River.

parks.name: Stone State Park
parks.description: Loess Hills park with sweeping prairie views.

motto: Our liberties we prize and our rights we will maintain

quarter.design: A one-room schoolhouse with a teacher and students planting a tree, inspired by an artwork by Grant Wood, with the words 'Foundation in Education'.

funFacts: Iowa is the only state whose east and west borders are both formed entirely by rivers — the Mississippi and the Missouri.
Iowa produces more corn than any other state, growing about 2.5 billion bushels each year.
The famous painting 'American Gothic' was painted by Iowan Grant Wood in 1930.

flag.description: Three vertical stripes of blue, white, and red (inspired by the French tricolor honoring the Louisiana Purchase) with a bald eagle carrying a ribbon at the center.

name: Kansas

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: A flat sea of grass that stretches all the way to the Rocky Mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: Kansas grows more wheat than any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Arkansas River
physicalFeatures.description: A long, sandy river that flows from the Rockies clear across Kansas.
physicalFeatures.fact: Kansans pronounce it 'ar-KAN-zus' even though Arkansas the state is 'AR-kan-saw.'

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri forms a short stretch of Kansas's northeast border.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the longest river in the United States.

physicalFeatures.name: Flint Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Rolling hills covered in the last large stretch of tallgrass prairie left on Earth.
physicalFeatures.fact: Tallgrass once stood taller than a person on horseback.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Sunflower
physicalFeatures.description: Kansas's highest point — a gentle rise in a wheat field near the Colorado border.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 4,039 feet, it's marked by a sunflower made of railroad spikes.

physicalFeatures.name: Cimarron National Grassland
physicalFeatures.description: A vast prairie preserve where the Santa Fe Trail wagon ruts still show.
physicalFeatures.fact: You can still see 150-year-old wagon tracks pressed into the grass.

parks.name: Tallgrass Prairie National Preserve
parks.description: One of the last large pieces of original tallgrass prairie in America.

parks.name: Nicodemus National Historic Site
parks.description: A town founded by formerly enslaved Black settlers after the Civil War.

parks.name: Kanopolis State Park
parks.description: Kansas's first state park, with red sandstone canyons and a big lake.

parks.name: Clinton State Park
parks.description: A big reservoir surrounded by woods near Lawrence.

parks.name: Fort Larned National Historic Site
parks.description: A well-preserved frontier army fort that once guarded the Santa Fe Trail.

parks.name: Brown v. Board of Education National Historical Park
parks.description: School at the heart of the landmark civil rights case.

parks.name: Monument Rocks
parks.description: Chalk formations in the western Kansas prairie.

parks.name: Wilson State Park
parks.description: Park surrounding 'the clearest lake in Kansas'.

parks.name: Tuttle Creek State Park
parks.description: Big reservoir park great for boating and camping.

parks.name: Cheyenne Bottoms
parks.description: Critical wetland stopover for migrating birds.

parks.name: Castle Rock
parks.description: Towering chalk pillar in the high plains.

quarter.design: An American bison standing in front of a field of sunflowers, two of the state's most famous symbols.

funFacts: Kansas is one of the leading wheat-producing states and is often called the 'Breadbasket of America'.
The geographic center of the lower 48 states is in Lebanon, Kansas.
Dorothy and Toto from 'The Wizard of Oz' are from Kansas, and the state really does see many tornadoes each year.

flag.description: A dark blue field showing the state seal above a sunflower and the word 'KANSAS', with the seal depicting a settler's cabin, plowed fields, buffalo, and 34 stars.

name: Kentucky

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Steep, forested mountains and hollows fill the eastern third of Kentucky.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some of America's oldest mountains, worn down by hundreds of millions of years.

physicalFeatures.name: Ohio River
physicalFeatures.description: The Ohio River forms Kentucky's entire northern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries more cargo by barge than any other river in the country.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The Mississippi clips Kentucky's western tip on its journey south.
physicalFeatures.fact: An 1811 earthquake made part of the Mississippi run backward in Kentucky.

physicalFeatures.name: Cumberland Plateau
physicalFeatures.description: A high, flat-topped land of forests, sandstone arches, and waterfalls.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its sandstone has been carved into more than 100 natural rock arches.

physicalFeatures.name: Black Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Kentucky's highest point, deep in the Appalachian coal country.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 4,145 feet, it sits right on the Virginia border.

physicalFeatures.name: Mammoth Cave System
physicalFeatures.description: The longest cave system on Earth, with passages going on for hundreds of miles.
physicalFeatures.fact: More than 420 miles of cave have been mapped — and there's more to find.

parks.name: Mammoth Cave National Park
parks.description: The world's longest cave system, with tours through huge underground rooms.

parks.name: Cumberland Gap National Historical Park
parks.description: The mountain pass that pioneers used to cross into Kentucky.

parks.name: Land Between the Lakes National Recreation Area
parks.description: A long peninsula between two giant lakes, full of wild bison and elk.

parks.name: Natural Bridge State Resort Park
parks.description: A 78-foot-long sandstone arch you can walk across.

parks.name: Red River Gorge
parks.description: A maze of canyons, cliffs, and over 100 natural stone arches.

parks.name: Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historical Park
parks.description: The Kentucky cabin site where Lincoln was born.

parks.name: Big South Fork National River
parks.description: Wild river canyon with natural arches and old coal mines.

parks.name: Cumberland Falls State Resort Park
parks.description: 'Niagara of the South' famous for its rare moonbow.

parks.name: Kentucky Horse Park
parks.description: Working horse farm and museum celebrating all things horse.

parks.name: Carter Caves State Resort Park
parks.description: More than 20 caves and a natural bridge.

parks.name: Lake Cumberland State Resort Park
parks.description: Houseboat capital of the world on a huge man-made lake.

motto: United we stand, divided we fall

quarter.design: A thoroughbred racehorse behind a fence with a stately mansion in the background, with the words 'My Old Kentucky Home'.

funFacts: Mammoth Cave in Kentucky is the longest known cave system in the world, with more than 420 miles explored.
The Kentucky Derby horse race has been run at Churchill Downs in Louisville every year since 1875.
Fort Knox in Kentucky stores most of the United States' gold reserves.

flag.description: A navy blue field with the state seal at the center, showing two men—a frontiersman and a statesman—embracing, with the state motto 'United We Stand, Divided We Fall'.

name: Louisiana

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The mighty Mississippi snakes through Louisiana on its way to the Gulf.
physicalFeatures.fact: New Orleans sits below the level of the river, protected by huge earth walls.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River Delta
physicalFeatures.description: A vast network of muddy channels where the river finally meets the sea.
physicalFeatures.fact: The delta grows or shrinks every year as the river drops new mud.

physicalFeatures.name: Atchafalaya Basin
physicalFeatures.description: The largest river swamp in the United States, full of cypress trees and alligators.
physicalFeatures.fact: It covers nearly a million acres of swamp and bayou.

physicalFeatures.name: Gulf of Mexico Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A maze of marshes, bays, and bayous along Louisiana's southern edge.
physicalFeatures.fact: Louisiana loses a football field of coast to the sea every 100 minutes.

physicalFeatures.name: Driskill Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Louisiana's highest point, a tree-covered hill in the north of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 535 feet, it's actually shorter than many Louisiana office buildings.

physicalFeatures.name: Red River
physicalFeatures.description: A reddish-brown river named for the color of clay it picks up.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its red color comes from soil washed in all the way from Texas.

parks.name: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park
parks.description: Six sites celebrating Louisiana's swamps, Cajun culture, and pirate history.

parks.name: Cane River Creole National Historical Park
parks.description: Two old cotton plantations along the historic Cane River.

parks.name: Kisatchie National Forest
parks.description: Louisiana's only national forest, with longleaf pine and clear creeks.

parks.name: Fontainebleau State Park
parks.description: Lakefront marshland with old sugar mill ruins to explore.

parks.name: Poverty Point National Monument
parks.description: Mysterious giant earthen ridges built by Native peoples 3,400 years ago.

parks.name: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve
parks.description: Cajun bayou wilderness named for a famous pirate.

parks.name: New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park
parks.description: Celebrates the birthplace of jazz music.

parks.name: Chicot State Park
parks.description: Louisiana's largest state park, with a cypress-tupelo lake.

parks.name: Bogue Chitto State Park
parks.description: Sandbars and bluffs along a meandering blackwater river.

parks.name: Tickfaw State Park
parks.description: Five different ecosystems connected by boardwalks.

parks.name: Lake Fausse Pointe State Park
parks.description: Bald cypress swamp once part of the Atchafalaya Basin.

quarter.design: A pelican, a trumpet with musical notes, and the outline of the Louisiana Purchase territory, with the words 'Louisiana Purchase'.

funFacts: Louisiana is the only U.S. state that uses parishes instead of counties.
New Orleans is the birthplace of jazz music, which began in the early 1900s.
The Louisiana Purchase in 1803 doubled the size of the United States by buying land from France for about 3 cents an acre.

flag.description: A blue field with a white pelican feeding her three chicks with drops of her own blood (a 'pelican in her piety'), above a banner with the state motto 'Union, Justice, Confidence'.

name: Maine

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A rocky, jagged coast with countless coves, peninsulas, and islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Straight-line, Maine's coast is 228 miles — but follow every curve and it's 3,500.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Katahdin
physicalFeatures.description: Maine's tallest mountain and the northern end of the Appalachian Trail.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'greatest mountain' in the Penobscot language.

physicalFeatures.name: Penobscot River
physicalFeatures.description: Maine's longest river, flowing from the north woods to the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: Atlantic salmon swim up it from the ocean to lay their eggs.

physicalFeatures.name: Acadia coastline
physicalFeatures.description: A famous stretch of pink granite shoreline pounded by Atlantic waves.
physicalFeatures.fact: Cadillac Mountain on this coast is the first U.S. spot to see sunrise each day.

physicalFeatures.name: Maine North Woods
physicalFeatures.description: A vast, mostly empty forest of spruce, fir, and pine in northern Maine.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the largest unbroken forest east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Moosehead Lake
physicalFeatures.description: Maine's largest lake, shaped a bit like a moose's head from above.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so big that there are more than 80 islands inside it.

parks.name: Acadia National Park
parks.description: Rocky coastlines, granite peaks, and quiet forests on Mount Desert Island.

parks.name: Katahdin Woods and Waters National Monument
parks.description: Wild forests, rivers, and views of Mount Katahdin to the south.

parks.name: Baxter State Park
parks.description: A vast wilderness park with Maine's tallest mountain at its center.

parks.name: Camden Hills State Park
parks.description: Coastal mountains with a famous view of Penobscot Bay from above.

parks.name: Saint Croix Island International Historic Site
parks.description: The site of one of the earliest French settlements in North America.

parks.name: Reid State Park
parks.description: Sandy ocean beach with tide pools.

parks.name: Two Lights State Park
parks.description: Rocky Atlantic coast near famous twin lighthouses.

parks.name: Wolfe's Neck Woods State Park
parks.description: Coastal forest with osprey nests.

parks.name: Quoddy Head State Park
parks.description: Easternmost point in the contiguous U.S., with a candy-striped lighthouse.

parks.name: Sebago Lake State Park
parks.description: Maine's second-largest lake, great for swimming.

parks.name: Grafton Notch State Park
parks.description: Waterfalls and gorges in western Maine.

quarter.design: The Pemaquid Point Lighthouse on a rocky coast with a schooner sailing in the background.

funFacts: Maine produces about 90 percent of the lobster caught in the United States.
Maine has more coastline than California, with about 3,500 miles when you count all the bays and islands.
About 89 percent of Maine is covered in forest — more than any other state.

flag.description: A blue field bearing the state coat of arms: a moose resting beneath a tall pine tree, flanked by a farmer and a sailor representing Maine's land and sea heritage.

name: Maryland

physicalFeatures.name: Chesapeake Bay
physicalFeatures.description: The largest estuary in the United States, full of crabs, oysters, and ships.
physicalFeatures.fact: It was formed when an asteroid hit Earth 35 million years ago.

physicalFeatures.name: Potomac River
physicalFeatures.description: A long river forming much of Maryland's southern border with Virginia.
physicalFeatures.fact: It flows past the U.S. Capitol on its way to the Chesapeake Bay.

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Forested ridges and valleys cover Maryland's narrow western panhandle.
physicalFeatures.fact: Maryland is only 1.9 miles wide at one spot in these mountains.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A short but sandy 30-mile stretch of Atlantic beach in eastern Maryland.
physicalFeatures.fact: Wild ponies roam along Maryland's Assateague Island beach.

physicalFeatures.name: Backbone Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Maryland's highest point, in the wooded western tip of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 3,360 feet, it's the only Maryland mountain over 3,000 feet.

physicalFeatures.name: Susquehanna River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that empties huge amounts of fresh water into Chesapeake Bay.
physicalFeatures.fact: It provides about half of all the fresh water in the bay.

parks.name: Assateague Island National Seashore
parks.description: A wild barrier island where free-roaming ponies live on the beach.

parks.name: Catoctin Mountain Park
parks.description: Forested mountains that contain the President's Camp David retreat.

parks.name: Antietam National Battlefield
parks.description: A peaceful field that was once the site of the bloodiest day of the Civil War.

parks.name: Deep Creek Lake State Park
parks.description: Maryland's largest freshwater lake, deep in the western mountains.

parks.name: Fort McHenry National Monument
parks.description: The star-shaped fort whose flag inspired the U.S. national anthem.

parks.name: Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park
parks.description: Honors the famous abolitionist born in this region.

parks.name: Patapsco Valley State Park
parks.description: Maryland's first state park with hiking, waterfalls, and history.

parks.name: Swallow Falls State Park
parks.description: Home to Maryland's tallest free-falling waterfall.

parks.name: Calvert Cliffs State Park
parks.description: Beach famous for finding 15-million-year-old shark teeth.

parks.name: Rocky Gap State Park
parks.description: Mountain lake park with a deep gorge.

quarter.design: The dome of the Maryland Statehouse with white oak leaf clusters, and a banner reading 'The Old Line State'.

funFacts: The Chesapeake Bay in Maryland is the largest estuary in the United States.
Francis Scott Key wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner', America's national anthem, in Baltimore Harbor during the War of 1812.
Maryland is sometimes called 'America in Miniature' because it has mountains, beaches, farmland, and forests in a small area.

flag.description: A bold heraldic banner combining the black-and-gold checkered arms of the Calvert family with the red-and-white quartered cross of the Crossland family.

name: Massachusetts

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A rocky, sandy coast packed with historic ports, beaches, and lighthouses.
physicalFeatures.fact: Massachusetts has over 1,500 miles of tidal shoreline.

physicalFeatures.name: Cape Cod
physicalFeatures.description: A curved sandy peninsula that sticks 60 miles out into the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: From space, Cape Cod looks like a giant flexed arm.

physicalFeatures.name: Berkshire Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Quiet, rolling forested hills in the western part of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: They blaze with bright reds and oranges every fall.

physicalFeatures.name: Connecticut River
physicalFeatures.description: New England's longest river cuts through the heart of Massachusetts.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its valley has Massachusetts's richest farmland.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Greylock
physicalFeatures.description: Massachusetts's highest point, with views of five states from the top.
physicalFeatures.fact: An author wrote 'Moby-Dick' looking at this mountain because it looked like a whale.

physicalFeatures.name: Charles River
physicalFeatures.description: A winding river that separates Boston from Cambridge.
physicalFeatures.fact: It changes direction four times before reaching the Atlantic.

parks.name: Cape Cod National Seashore
parks.description: 40 miles of protected Atlantic beach, dunes, and salt marsh.

parks.name: Boston Harbor Islands National Recreation Area
parks.description: Over 30 islands in Boston Harbor, reached by ferry from downtown.

parks.name: Minute Man National Historical Park
parks.description: The site where the American Revolution began on April 19, 1775.

parks.name: Walden Pond State Reservation
parks.description: The clear, deep kettle pond where Henry David Thoreau lived in a small cabin.

parks.name: Boston National Historical Park
parks.description: Eight historic sites linked by the famous Freedom Trail.

parks.name: Lowell National Historical Park
parks.description: America's first planned industrial city.

parks.name: Adams National Historical Park
parks.description: Homes of Presidents John Adams and John Quincy Adams.

parks.name: Salem Maritime National Historic Site
parks.description: Tall ship and wharves of America's first national historic site.

parks.name: Mount Greylock State Reservation
parks.description: Massachusetts's highest peak with a stone war memorial tower.

parks.name: Mount Washington State Forest
parks.description: Home to Bash Bish, Massachusetts's tallest waterfall.

parks.name: Nickerson State Park
parks.description: Cape Cod park with eight crystal-clear kettle ponds.

motto: Ense petit placidam sub libertate quietem

quarter.design: An outline of the state with a Minute Man statue, honoring the 1775 Revolutionary War soldiers, and the words 'The Bay State'.

funFacts: The first Thanksgiving was held in Plymouth, Massachusetts in 1621.
Boston has the oldest subway system in the United States, opened in 1897.
Harvard University, founded in 1636, is the oldest college in the United States.

flag.description: A white field with the state coat of arms at center: a Native American Algonquian figure holding a bow and arrow under a star, on a blue shield with a sword above.

name: Michigan

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Superior
physicalFeatures.description: The largest freshwater lake on Earth by surface area.
physicalFeatures.fact: It holds 10% of all the fresh surface water in the world.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Michigan
physicalFeatures.description: The only Great Lake entirely within the United States.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its sand dunes on the eastern shore are the largest freshwater dunes on Earth.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Huron
physicalFeatures.description: A Great Lake on Michigan's east, with thousands of small islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: It contains Manitoulin — the world's largest island in a freshwater lake.

physicalFeatures.name: Upper Peninsula
physicalFeatures.description: Michigan's wild northern peninsula, covered in pine forests and waterfalls.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's bigger than several U.S. states put together.

physicalFeatures.name: Sleeping Bear Dunes
physicalFeatures.description: Towering sand dunes that rise 450 feet above Lake Michigan.
physicalFeatures.fact: You can run down them, but the climb back up takes about an hour.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Arvon
physicalFeatures.description: Michigan's highest point, a tree-covered peak in the Upper Peninsula.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,979 feet, it was only proven to be the highest spot in 1982.

parks.name: Isle Royale National Park
parks.description: A remote wilderness island in Lake Superior, reached only by boat or seaplane.

parks.name: Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore
parks.description: Massive sand dunes towering over Lake Michigan.

parks.name: Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore
parks.description: Multicolored sandstone cliffs along the shore of Lake Superior.

parks.name: Tahquamenon Falls State Park
parks.description: One of the largest waterfalls east of the Mississippi, with root-beer-colored water.

parks.name: Mackinac Island State Park
parks.description: A car-free island where horses and bicycles are the only way to get around.

parks.name: Keweenaw National Historical Park
parks.description: Tells the story of Michigan's copper mining boom.

parks.name: Porcupine Mountains Wilderness State Park
parks.description: Old-growth forest and the famous Lake of the Clouds.

parks.name: Holland State Park
parks.description: Beach park with Michigan's most famous lighthouse, 'Big Red'.

parks.name: Ludington State Park
parks.description: Big Sable Point Lighthouse and rolling sand dunes.

parks.name: Warren Dunes State Park
parks.description: Towering 260-foot dunes along Lake Michigan.

quarter.design: An outline of the state showing both peninsulas, with all five Great Lakes labeled around it.

funFacts: Michigan touches four of the five Great Lakes and has more freshwater coastline than any other state.
Michigan is shaped like a mitten, and locals often point to a spot on their hand to show where they live.
Detroit is known as the 'Motor City' because Henry Ford built the first mass-produced cars there.

flag.description: A blue field bearing the state coat of arms with an elk and a moose holding a shield, an eagle above, and the state mottos 'Tuebor' (I will defend) and 'Si Quaeris Peninsulam Amoenam Circumspice'.

name: Minnesota

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Superior
physicalFeatures.description: The biggest of the Great Lakes laps Minnesota's rocky northern shore.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so big that it has its own measurable tides.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: America's mightiest river starts as a small stream in northern Minnesota.
physicalFeatures.fact: At its source you can step across it with one stride.

physicalFeatures.name: Boundary Waters
physicalFeatures.description: A wilderness of more than 1,000 lakes connected by canoe routes.
physicalFeatures.fact: There are no roads — you have to paddle and portage to get around.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Flat, fertile prairie covers western Minnesota's farmland.
physicalFeatures.fact: Minnesota is one of the country's biggest growers of sugar beets.

physicalFeatures.name: Eagle Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Minnesota's highest point, a quiet wooded summit near Lake Superior.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's only 13 miles from the state's lowest point, Lake Superior's shore.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake of the Woods
physicalFeatures.description: A huge lake on the Canadian border with more than 14,500 islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has more shoreline than Lake Superior, even though it's much smaller.

parks.name: Voyageurs National Park
parks.description: A water-based park you explore by boat through a maze of forested lakes.

parks.name: Mississippi National River and Recreation Area
parks.description: A 72-mile stretch of the Mississippi through the Twin Cities.

parks.name: Itasca State Park
parks.description: Where the Mississippi River begins — you can walk across it on stones.

parks.name: Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness
parks.description: A million-acre wilderness of lakes and forests you explore only by canoe.

parks.name: Pipestone National Monument
parks.description: Sacred quarries where Native peoples have carved pipestone for centuries.

parks.name: Grand Portage National Monument
parks.description: Old fur trade fort on Lake Superior.

parks.name: Gooseberry Falls State Park
parks.description: Series of beautiful waterfalls along Lake Superior's North Shore.

parks.name: Tettegouche State Park
parks.description: Cliffs, waterfalls, and inland lakes on Lake Superior.

parks.name: Split Rock Lighthouse State Park
parks.description: Iconic 1910 lighthouse atop a 130-foot cliff.

parks.name: Jay Cooke State Park
parks.description: St. Louis River rapids and a famous swinging bridge.

parks.name: Fort Snelling State Park
parks.description: Where the Minnesota and Mississippi rivers meet.

quarter.design: Two people fishing on a lake with a loon floating in the foreground, plus a forest and the state's outline, with the words 'Land of 10,000 Lakes'.

funFacts: Minnesota actually has more than 11,000 lakes, not just 10,000 as the nickname suggests.
The Mississippi River starts in Minnesota at Lake Itasca, where you can walk across it on stepping stones.
The first Scotch tape, Post-it Notes, and water skis were all invented in Minnesota.

flag.description: A dark blue eight-pointed star on a lighter blue field, with a horizontal stripe of pale blue along the fly side, adopted in 2024 to replace the previous seal-on-blue design.

name: Mississippi

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The river the state is named for forms its entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Mark Twain wrote his most famous stories about boats on this river.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi Delta
physicalFeatures.description: A flat, fertile floodplain along the river — some of the richest farm soil in America.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its rich soil grows much of America's cotton.

physicalFeatures.name: Gulf of Mexico Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Warm beaches and barrier islands along the state's southern edge.
physicalFeatures.fact: The mainland beach is human-made, with sand trucked in from offshore.

physicalFeatures.name: Pearl River
physicalFeatures.description: A winding river that runs from Mississippi to the Louisiana coast.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name comes from the pearls once found inside its freshwater mussels.

physicalFeatures.name: Woodall Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Mississippi's highest point, a quiet wooded hill in the northeast.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 807 feet, it's the lowest 'highest point' of any state east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: De Soto National Forest
physicalFeatures.description: A great pine forest where longleaf pines and red-cockaded woodpeckers live.
physicalFeatures.fact: It covers more than 500,000 acres of southern pine country.

parks.name: Vicksburg National Military Park
