parks.description: The site of a key Civil War siege along the Mississippi River.

parks.name: Gulf Islands National Seashore
parks.description: A string of barrier islands with white-sand beaches and sea turtles.

parks.name: Natchez Trace Parkway
parks.description: A 444-mile scenic road along an ancient Native American trail.

parks.name: Tishomingo State Park
parks.description: Massive sandstone bluffs and a swinging bridge over a clear stream.

parks.name: Brices Cross Roads National Battlefield Site
parks.description: A small, peaceful battlefield from the Civil War.

parks.name: Natchez National Historical Park
parks.description: Three sites telling Natchez's antebellum and African American history.

parks.name: Tupelo National Battlefield
parks.description: Small park marking an 1864 Civil War battle.

parks.name: Wall Doxey State Park
parks.description: Spring-fed lake surrounded by towering hardwoods.

parks.name: Percy Quin State Park
parks.description: Lakeside park with a championship golf course.

parks.name: Roosevelt State Park
parks.description: Forested hills around Shadow Lake.

parks.name: Buccaneer State Park
parks.description: Gulf Coast park named for the pirate Jean Lafitte.

quarter.design: Two magnolia blossoms with the words 'The Magnolia State' on a banner.

funFacts: The Mississippi River, which forms the state's western border, is the second-longest river in the U.S.
Root beer was invented in Biloxi, Mississippi in 1898.
The teddy bear got its name from President Theodore Roosevelt after a famous hunting trip in Mississippi in 1902.

flag.description: A red, blue, and gold flag featuring a magnolia blossom (the state flower) encircled by 21 white stars and one larger gold star, adopted in 2021 after retiring the prior Confederate-themed design.

name: Missouri

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The Mississippi flows past St. Louis on its long journey to the Gulf.
physicalFeatures.fact: St. Louis was once the busiest port in the world after New Orleans.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The longest river in the United States meets the Mississippi at St. Louis.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries so much mud it was nicknamed the 'Big Muddy.'

physicalFeatures.name: Ozark Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Forested mountains and clear, spring-fed streams cover southern Missouri.
physicalFeatures.fact: They have more caves than almost any other part of America — over 6,000.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake of the Ozarks
physicalFeatures.description: A huge lake shaped like a dragon, with over 1,100 miles of shoreline.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has more shoreline than the entire California coast.

physicalFeatures.name: Taum Sauk Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: Missouri's highest point, deep in the rocky Saint Francois Mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,772 feet, it's covered with some of the oldest rock in North America.

physicalFeatures.name: Current River
physicalFeatures.description: A clear spring-fed river beloved by canoers and tubers.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's one of the few rivers in America protected as a 'national scenic river.'

parks.name: Gateway Arch National Park
parks.description: The 630-foot stainless steel arch celebrating America's westward expansion.

parks.name: Ozark National Scenic Riverways
parks.description: The first U.S. national park created to protect a wild river.

parks.name: Wilson's Creek National Battlefield
parks.description: The site of the first major Civil War battle west of the Mississippi.

parks.name: Ha Ha Tonka State Park
parks.description: A stone castle ruin perched above a deep blue spring.

parks.name: Mark Twain National Forest
parks.description: A vast forest of Ozark hills, hollows, and clear streams.

parks.name: George Washington Carver National Monument
parks.description: Birthplace of the famous African American scientist.

parks.name: Harry S Truman National Historic Site
parks.description: The home of America's 33rd president.

parks.name: Elephant Rocks State Park
parks.description: Giant pink granite boulders that look like a herd of elephants.

parks.name: Johnson's Shut-Ins State Park
parks.description: Natural water-park canyon carved through volcanic rock.

parks.name: Meramec State Park
parks.description: More than 40 caves along the Meramec River.

parks.name: Roaring River State Park
parks.description: Spring-fed trout stream in a steep Ozark valley.

quarter.design: Lewis and Clark and Sacagawea's son Pomp returning down the Missouri River, with the Gateway Arch in the background and the words 'Corps of Discovery'.

funFacts: The Gateway Arch in St. Louis is the tallest monument in the United States at 630 feet.
Missouri is where Lewis and Clark started their famous expedition west in 1804.
The ice cream cone was invented at the 1904 World's Fair in St. Louis.

flag.description: Three horizontal stripes of red, white, and blue (echoing the U.S. flag and France) with the state seal at the center surrounded by 24 stars.

name: Montana

physicalFeatures.name: Rocky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: The Rockies cover Montana's western third with snow-capped peaks.
physicalFeatures.fact: More than 100 mountain ranges are inside Montana's borders.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri begins in Montana where three rivers come together.
physicalFeatures.fact: Lewis and Clark followed it across the state on their famous expedition.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Vast, open grasslands cover the eastern two-thirds of Montana.
physicalFeatures.fact: Cattle outnumber people in Montana by nearly three to one.

physicalFeatures.name: Yellowstone River
physicalFeatures.description: The longest free-flowing river in the lower 48 states.
physicalFeatures.fact: It runs 692 miles without a single dam blocking it.

physicalFeatures.name: Granite Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Montana's highest mountain, a steep granite spire in the Beartooth Mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 12,807 feet, it's one of the hardest U.S. state high points to climb.

physicalFeatures.name: Flathead Lake
physicalFeatures.description: The largest natural freshwater lake west of the Mississippi.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so clear you can see 60 feet down on a calm day.

parks.name: Glacier National Park
parks.description: Snowy peaks, blue lakes, and herds of mountain goats along the Canadian border.

parks.name: Yellowstone National Park
parks.description: A slice of America's first national park, with geysers and grizzly bears.

parks.name: Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument
parks.description: The site of Custer's famous last stand on the Great Plains.

parks.name: Makoshika State Park
parks.description: Montana's largest state park, full of badlands and dinosaur bones.

parks.name: Bighorn Canyon National Recreation Area
parks.description: A canyon over 1,000 feet deep filled with the blue waters of a reservoir.

parks.name: Big Hole National Battlefield
parks.description: Honors the Nez Perce War of 1877.

parks.name: Grant-Kohrs Ranch National Historic Site
parks.description: A working cattle ranch preserving the cowboy era.

parks.name: Lewis and Clark Caverns State Park
parks.description: One of the largest limestone caverns in the Northwest.

parks.name: Bannack State Park
parks.description: Montana's first gold-rush ghost town.

parks.name: Flathead Lake State Park
parks.description: Largest natural freshwater lake west of the Mississippi.

parks.name: Pictograph Cave State Park
parks.description: Ancient rock paintings made by Native Americans.

quarter.design: A bison skull above a landscape of rivers, mountains, and forests, representing Montana's wilderness and Native American heritage.

funFacts: Montana is sometimes called 'Big Sky Country' because of its huge open spaces and sweeping views.
Glacier National Park has more than 130 named lakes and 700 miles of hiking trails.
More dinosaur fossils have been found in Montana than in any other state.

flag.description: A blue field with the state seal showing mountains, the Great Falls of the Missouri River, and a plow, shovel, and pick, with the word 'MONTANA' added in 1981.

name: Nebraska

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Endless flat farmland covers most of Nebraska.
physicalFeatures.fact: Nebraska's nickname 'Cornhusker State' comes from its enormous corn fields.

physicalFeatures.name: Platte River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide, shallow river often called 'a mile wide and an inch deep.'
physicalFeatures.fact: Half a million sandhill cranes stop here every spring on their migration.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri forms Nebraska's entire eastern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Lewis and Clark traveled this whole border on their way west in 1804.

physicalFeatures.name: Sand Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Vast rolling sand dunes covered in prairie grass — the largest dune field in the Americas.
physicalFeatures.fact: They cover an area larger than the state of West Virginia.

physicalFeatures.name: Panorama Point
physicalFeatures.description: Nebraska's highest point, a quiet rise on a buffalo and longhorn farm.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 5,424 feet, it's higher than many mountains east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Chimney Rock
physicalFeatures.description: A tall stone spire that pioneers used as a landmark on the Oregon Trail.
physicalFeatures.fact: It rises nearly 300 feet above the Platte River valley.

parks.name: Homestead National Historical Park
parks.description: Honors the pioneers who claimed free farmland under the Homestead Act.

parks.name: Scotts Bluff National Monument
parks.description: Towering bluffs that pioneers used as a landmark on the Oregon Trail.

parks.name: Agate Fossil Beds National Monument
parks.description: A hillside packed with bones of ancient mammals from 20 million years ago.

parks.name: Chimney Rock National Historic Site
parks.description: A famous stone spire that guided pioneers along the Oregon Trail.

parks.name: Niobrara National Scenic River
parks.description: A clear, sandy river famous for canoeing past dozens of small waterfalls.

parks.name: Indian Cave State Park
parks.description: Sandstone bluffs with ancient Native American petroglyphs.

parks.name: Platte River State Park
parks.description: Wooded park on cliffs above the Platte River.

parks.name: Ponca State Park
parks.description: Bluffs and oak forests on the Missouri River.

parks.name: Fort Robinson State Park
parks.description: Historic frontier army post turned outdoor playground.

parks.name: Eugene T. Mahoney State Park
parks.description: Family park with a giant observation tower and water playground.

quarter.design: Chimney Rock, a famous landmark on the Oregon Trail, with a covered wagon and pioneers traveling beside it under the sun.

funFacts: Nebraska is the only state in the U.S. with a one-house (unicameral) legislature.
The Sandhills cover one-fourth of Nebraska and are one of the largest sand dune regions in the Western Hemisphere.
Arbor Day was started in Nebraska City in 1872 — a holiday all about planting trees.

flag.description: A blue field bearing the state seal in gold and silver, depicting a steamboat on the Missouri River, a blacksmith, a settler's cabin, and the Rocky Mountains in the distance.

name: Nevada

physicalFeatures.name: Great Basin Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A high cold desert of sagebrush and snow-capped mountain islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Rain that falls here never reaches the ocean — it just sinks or dries up.

physicalFeatures.name: Mojave Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A lower, hotter desert in Nevada's southern tip, near Las Vegas.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's home to Joshua trees, which can live for over 150 years.

physicalFeatures.name: Sierra Nevada
physicalFeatures.description: A snowy mountain range along Nevada's western border with California.
physicalFeatures.fact: Snowmelt from these mountains supplies most of Nevada's drinking water.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Tahoe
physicalFeatures.description: A huge blue lake half in Nevada, half in California, high in the mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its water is so clear you can see down 70 feet.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Mead
physicalFeatures.description: America's largest man-made lake, formed when Hoover Dam blocked the Colorado River.
physicalFeatures.fact: It can hold enough water to cover the state of Pennsylvania ankle-deep.

physicalFeatures.name: Boundary Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Nevada's highest mountain, right on the California border.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 13,147 feet, its summit is a steep scramble over loose rock.

parks.name: Great Basin National Park
parks.description: Bristlecone pines, glacier-carved valleys, and one of America's darkest night skies.

parks.name: Death Valley National Park
parks.description: A small piece of America's hottest desert reaches into Nevada.

parks.name: Lake Mead National Recreation Area
parks.description: Huge desert reservoirs created by Hoover Dam on the Colorado River.

parks.name: Valley of Fire State Park
parks.description: Bright red sandstone formations that seem to glow in the desert sun.

parks.name: Tule Springs Fossil Beds National Monument
parks.description: Bones of mammoths and giant ground sloths from the Ice Age, near Las Vegas.

parks.name: Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park
parks.description: Crystal-clear alpine lake on the Nevada-California border.

parks.name: Cathedral Gorge State Park
parks.description: Dramatic clay spires carved by erosion.

parks.name: Berlin-Ichthyosaur State Park
parks.description: Ghost town and 225-million-year-old marine reptile fossils.

parks.name: Spring Mountain Ranch State Park
parks.description: Historic ranch in Red Rock Canyon.

parks.name: Sand Harbor
parks.description: Lake Tahoe beach with crystal-clear coves.

quarter.design: Three wild mustangs galloping across the desert, with the sun rising behind mountains and sagebrush in the foreground.

funFacts: Nevada is the driest state in the U.S., with most of it covered by desert.
About 86 percent of the land in Nevada is owned by the federal government — more than any other state.
The Hoover Dam, on the Nevada-Arizona border, is one of the largest concrete structures in the world.

flag.description: A cobalt-blue field with a silver star, the word 'Nevada', and a wreath of sagebrush in the upper-left corner, plus the motto 'Battle Born' honoring Nevada's Civil War-era admission.

name: New Hampshire

physicalFeatures.name: White Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A rugged range of granite peaks in the north of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some of the world's worst weather is recorded here on Mount Washington.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Washington
physicalFeatures.description: The tallest peak in the northeast, famous for sudden, violent storms.
physicalFeatures.fact: Wind speeds on the summit once hit 231 mph — a world record for over 60 years.

physicalFeatures.name: Connecticut River
physicalFeatures.description: The Connecticut forms New Hampshire's entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Even though it's the border, the river belongs entirely to New Hampshire.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: New Hampshire has the shortest ocean coastline of any U.S. state on the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's only 18 miles long — you could walk it in a day.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Winnipesaukee
physicalFeatures.description: New Hampshire's largest lake, dotted with hundreds of islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'smile of the great spirit' in Abenaki.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Monadnock
physicalFeatures.description: A lone mountain that rises high above the surrounding forest.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's said to be one of the most-climbed mountains in the world.

parks.name: Saint-Gaudens National Historical Park
parks.description: The home and studio of a famous American sculptor.

parks.name: White Mountain National Forest
parks.description: Almost 800,000 acres of granite peaks, waterfalls, and bear-filled woods.

parks.name: Franconia Notch State Park
parks.description: A dramatic mountain pass with cliffs, waterfalls, and a granite flume.

parks.name: Mount Monadnock State Park
parks.description: A bare-topped mountain with views of all six New England states from the summit.

parks.name: Crawford Notch State Park
parks.description: A deep pass through the White Mountains with thundering waterfalls.

parks.name: Mount Washington State Park
parks.description: Highest peak in the Northeast, with the world's worst weather.

parks.name: Echo Lake State Park
parks.description: Mountain lake with the famous Cathedral Ledge.

parks.name: Pawtuckaway State Park
parks.description: Glacial boulder field and a stone tower.

parks.name: Bear Brook State Park
parks.description: New Hampshire's largest state park with miles of trails.

parks.name: Hampton Beach State Park
parks.description: Popular Atlantic Ocean beach with a boardwalk.

parks.name: Odiorne Point State Park
parks.description: Rocky coastline with WWII bunkers and the Seacoast Science Center.

quarter.design: The 'Old Man of the Mountain', a famous rock formation that looked like a man's face, with nine stars and the state motto.

funFacts: Mount Washington in New Hampshire holds the record for one of the highest wind speeds ever recorded on land — 231 mph.
New Hampshire was the first colony to declare independence from England, six months before the Declaration of Independence.
The Old Man of the Mountain, a famous rock face that gave the state its symbol, fell down in 2003.

flag.description: A blue field with the state seal at the center: the frigate Raleigh under construction at Portsmouth in 1776, surrounded by laurel leaves and nine stars.

name: New Jersey

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: 130 miles of sandy beaches, boardwalks, and barrier islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Jersey Shore has more boardwalks than any other coast in America.

physicalFeatures.name: Pine Barrens
physicalFeatures.description: A vast sandy forest of pitch pine and tea-colored streams.
physicalFeatures.fact: It covers 22% of New Jersey — bigger than several U.S. national parks.

physicalFeatures.name: Delaware River
physicalFeatures.description: The Delaware forms New Jersey's entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: George Washington crossed it on Christmas night in 1776 — heading into Trenton.

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Forested ridges and valleys in New Jersey's northwest corner.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Appalachian Trail crosses 72 miles of New Jersey on its way to Maine.

physicalFeatures.name: High Point
physicalFeatures.description: New Jersey's highest point, marked by a 220-foot stone monument.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,803 feet, on a clear day you can see three states from the top.

physicalFeatures.name: Sandy Hook
physicalFeatures.description: A long, hook-shaped sand spit pointing toward New York Harbor.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has one of the oldest working lighthouses in the U.S. — built in 1764.

parks.name: Morristown National Historical Park
parks.description: Where George Washington's army camped through the harsh winter of 1779-80.

parks.name: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area
parks.description: A scenic river gap where the Delaware cuts through a mountain ridge.

parks.name: Pinelands National Reserve
parks.description: A million acres of protected pine forest, the first U.S. national reserve.

parks.name: Liberty State Park
parks.description: A waterfront park with the best views of the Statue of Liberty.

parks.name: Island Beach State Park
parks.description: Ten miles of unspoiled barrier island beach — no boardwalks, no buildings.

parks.name: Thomas Edison National Historical Park
parks.description: The laboratory of America's greatest inventor.

parks.name: Gateway National Recreation Area
parks.description: Beaches, forts, and Sandy Hook Lighthouse near New York Harbor.

parks.name: High Point State Park
parks.description: New Jersey's highest point, marked by a 220-foot obelisk.

parks.name: Wharton State Forest
parks.description: Largest tract of public land in NJ, in the Pine Barrens.

parks.name: Allaire State Park
parks.description: Restored 1830s iron-making village and steam railroad.

parks.name: Stokes State Forest
parks.description: Mountain forest crossed by the Appalachian Trail.

quarter.design: George Washington and his soldiers crossing the Delaware River on Christmas night in 1776, based on the famous painting.

funFacts: New Jersey is the most densely populated state in the U.S. — lots of people in a small space.
Thomas Edison did most of his most famous inventing in New Jersey, including the light bulb and the phonograph.
The first organized baseball game was played in Hoboken, New Jersey in 1846.

flag.description: A buff-colored field bearing the state coat of arms: a shield with three plows flanked by Liberty and Ceres, the goddess of agriculture, with a horse's head crest above.

name: New Mexico

physicalFeatures.name: Rio Grande
physicalFeatures.description: A great river that runs the full length of New Mexico from north to south.
physicalFeatures.fact: It eventually forms the border between Texas and Mexico.

physicalFeatures.name: Chihuahuan Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A high desert of yucca, cactus, and big open skies covers much of the south.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the largest desert in North America.

physicalFeatures.name: Sangre de Cristo Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Snow-capped mountains whose name means 'blood of Christ' in Spanish.
physicalFeatures.fact: They glow bright red at sunset, which is how they got their name.

physicalFeatures.name: White Sands
physicalFeatures.description: Snow-white sand dunes made of pure gypsum, sparkling in the sun.
physicalFeatures.fact: These are the largest gypsum dunes in the world.

physicalFeatures.name: Wheeler Peak
physicalFeatures.description: New Mexico's tallest mountain, with sweeping views of the Sangre de Cristos.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 13,167 feet, snow can linger on its summit even in July.

physicalFeatures.name: Carlsbad Caverns
physicalFeatures.description: A vast underground network of huge cave chambers and twisting tunnels.
physicalFeatures.fact: Hundreds of thousands of bats fly out of its main entrance each summer evening.

parks.name: Carlsbad Caverns National Park
parks.description: An enormous cave system with rooms big enough to fit football fields inside.

parks.name: White Sands National Park
parks.description: A wonderland of gleaming white gypsum dunes — perfect for sledding.

parks.name: Bandelier National Monument
parks.description: Ancient cliff dwellings carved into the soft volcanic rock of a canyon.

parks.name: Chaco Culture National Historical Park
parks.description: The ruins of an ancient Native American city in the middle of the desert.

parks.name: Petroglyph National Monument
parks.description: Thousands of rock carvings made by Native peoples and Spanish settlers.

parks.name: Aztec Ruins National Monument
parks.description: 900-year-old Ancestral Puebloan stone village.

parks.name: Gila Cliff Dwellings National Monument
parks.description: Cliff homes built by the Mogollon people 700 years ago.

parks.name: Valles Caldera National Preserve
parks.description: Giant collapsed volcano with elk-filled meadows.

parks.name: City of Rocks State Park
parks.description: Volcanic rock formations standing like a city.

parks.name: Bottomless Lakes State Park
parks.description: New Mexico's first state park, with deep blue sinkhole lakes.

quarter.design: The state outline with a Zia sun symbol, used by the indigenous Zia Pueblo people, and the words 'Land of Enchantment'.

funFacts: Santa Fe, New Mexico is the oldest state capital in the U.S., founded by the Spanish in 1610.
Carlsbad Caverns has a chamber so big it could fit six football fields inside it.
Albuquerque hosts the world's largest hot-air balloon festival every October.

flag.description: A red sun symbol of the Zia people centered on a golden yellow field, with the four groups of four rays representing the four directions, seasons, times of day, and stages of life.

name: New York

physicalFeatures.name: Adirondack Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A wild range of forested peaks and over 3,000 lakes in upstate New York.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Adirondack Park is bigger than Yellowstone, Yosemite, and Grand Canyon combined.

physicalFeatures.name: Hudson River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide, scenic river that flows past New York City to the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's a tidal river — the ocean tide reaches all the way to Albany.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Ontario
physicalFeatures.description: The smallest of the Great Lakes forms part of New York's northern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Niagara Falls drops into a river that flows from Lake Erie to Lake Ontario.

physicalFeatures.name: Finger Lakes
physicalFeatures.description: Eleven long, narrow lakes that look like fingers stretching across central New York.
physicalFeatures.fact: They were carved by glaciers during the last Ice Age.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: New York's coast includes Long Island and the busy harbor of New York City.
physicalFeatures.fact: Long Island Sound is over 100 miles long but never more than 21 miles wide.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Marcy
physicalFeatures.description: New York's highest mountain, deep in the Adirondacks.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 5,343 feet, it's the source of the mighty Hudson River.

physicalFeatures.name: Niagara Falls
physicalFeatures.description: One of the world's most famous waterfalls, on the border with Canada.
physicalFeatures.fact: Over 750,000 gallons of water plunge over the falls every second.

parks.name: Statue of Liberty National Monument
parks.description: The 305-foot-tall green statue welcoming visitors to New York Harbor.

parks.name: Gateway National Recreation Area
parks.description: Beaches, marshes, and historic forts surrounding New York Harbor.

parks.name: Niagara Falls State Park
parks.description: America's oldest state park, with thundering waterfalls right next to a city.

parks.name: Adirondack Park
parks.description: A 6-million-acre park, the largest publicly protected area in the lower 48.

parks.name: Fire Island National Seashore
parks.description: A long, narrow barrier island with sandy beaches and a wild deer population.

parks.name: Saratoga National Historical Park
parks.description: Site of a Revolutionary War turning point.

parks.name: Women's Rights National Historical Park
parks.description: Birthplace of the American women's rights movement.

parks.name: Letchworth State Park
parks.description: The 'Grand Canyon of the East' with three giant waterfalls.

parks.name: Watkins Glen State Park
parks.description: A two-mile gorge with 19 waterfalls.

parks.name: Catskill Park
parks.description: Forested mountain park with hiking and waterfalls.

quarter.design: The Statue of Liberty in front of an outline of the state with the Hudson River, and the words 'Gateway to Freedom'.

funFacts: New York City was the first capital of the United States after the Constitution was ratified.
Niagara Falls, on the New York-Canada border, is one of the most powerful waterfalls in the world by water flow.
The Adirondack Park in upstate New York is bigger than Yellowstone, Yosemite, and the Grand Canyon combined.

flag.description: A buff field bearing the state coat of arms: a shield showing a sunrise over the Hudson River, flanked by Liberty and Justice, with the state motto 'Excelsior' (Ever Upward) below.

name: North Carolina

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Old, rounded mountains covered in dense forests in the west.
physicalFeatures.fact: They include the highest mountains east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Smoky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Misty blue mountains shared with Tennessee, full of wildflowers and bears.
physicalFeatures.fact: More kinds of trees grow here than in all of Europe.

physicalFeatures.name: Outer Banks
physicalFeatures.description: A long chain of skinny barrier islands sticking out into the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Wright brothers chose this windy place for the first airplane flight in 1903.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A coast of sandy beaches, salty marshes, and wild ponies on barrier islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: More hurricanes hit the Outer Banks than almost any other U.S. coast.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Mitchell
physicalFeatures.description: The tallest mountain east of the Mississippi River.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 6,684 feet, it sometimes gets snow in June.

physicalFeatures.name: Cape Fear River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that flows through old swampy forests to the Atlantic.
physicalFeatures.fact: It got its scary name from rough waters at its mouth.

parks.name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
parks.description: The most-visited national park in America, full of misty peaks and waterfalls.

parks.name: Cape Hatteras National Seashore
parks.description: America's first national seashore, with its tall black-and-white lighthouse.

parks.name: Blue Ridge Parkway
parks.description: A 469-mile scenic mountain drive often called 'America's Favorite Drive.'

parks.name: Wright Brothers National Memorial
parks.description: The windy sand dune where the first airplane took flight in 1903.

parks.name: Cape Lookout National Seashore
parks.description: A wild barrier island reached only by ferry, with wild horses on the dunes.

parks.name: Carl Sandburg Home National Historic Site
parks.description: Home of the famous poet, with a goat dairy farm.

parks.name: Pisgah National Forest
parks.description: Birthplace of American forestry, with waterfalls everywhere.

parks.name: Hanging Rock State Park
parks.description: Granite peaks and waterfalls in the Sauratown Mountains.

parks.name: Mount Mitchell State Park
parks.description: Site of the highest peak east of the Mississippi.

parks.name: Pilot Mountain State Park
parks.description: A solitary stone pinnacle visible for miles.

parks.name: Jockey's Ridge State Park
parks.description: Tallest natural sand dune system on the East Coast.

quarter.design: The Wright Brothers' first airplane in flight at Kitty Hawk in 1903, with the words 'First Flight'.

funFacts: The Wright Brothers made the first successful airplane flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina in 1903.
Mount Mitchell in North Carolina is the highest peak east of the Mississippi River at 6,684 feet.
North Carolina was the first state in the U.S. to open a public university, the University of North Carolina, in 1795.

flag.description: A blue vertical stripe with a white 'N' and 'C' flanking a star, alongside horizontal stripes of red and white and two dates marking key moments in North Carolina's path to independence.

name: North Dakota

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Wide, flat grasslands stretch as far as you can see across most of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: North Dakota grows more wheat than almost any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri winds through North Dakota and was dammed to make Lake Sakakawea.
physicalFeatures.fact: Lewis and Clark spent their first winter on its banks in 1804.

physicalFeatures.name: Red River of the North
physicalFeatures.description: A river that flows backwards — north, into Canada — instead of south.
physicalFeatures.fact: It floods almost every spring when ice still blocks its northern end.

physicalFeatures.name: Badlands
physicalFeatures.description: Eroded buttes and colorful rock layers carved by wind and water.
physicalFeatures.fact: Theodore Roosevelt fell in love with this rugged landscape as a young man.

physicalFeatures.name: White Butte
physicalFeatures.description: North Dakota's highest point, a chalky white hill in the western badlands.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 3,506 feet, it sits on private ranchland.

physicalFeatures.name: Devils Lake
physicalFeatures.description: A salty lake that grows and shrinks with the weather.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has tripled in size since the 1990s and swallowed nearby roads.

parks.name: Theodore Roosevelt National Park
parks.description: Colorful badlands and herds of wild bison and prairie dogs.

parks.name: Knife River Indian Villages National Historic Site
parks.description: Round earthen lodges where Hidatsa and Mandan people lived for centuries.

parks.name: Fort Union Trading Post National Historic Site
parks.description: A rebuilt fur trading post on the upper Missouri River.

parks.name: Lake Sakakawea State Park
parks.description: A big reservoir park on one of America's largest man-made lakes.

parks.name: International Peace Garden
parks.description: A giant garden straddling the border with Canada, celebrating peace.

parks.name: Cross Ranch State Park
parks.description: One of the last free-flowing stretches of the Missouri River.

parks.name: Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park
parks.description: Reconstructed Custer house and Mandan earth lodges.

parks.name: Lewis and Clark State Park
parks.description: Park where Lewis and Clark camped along the Missouri.

parks.name: Icelandic State Park
parks.description: Pioneer history museum and Lake Renwick.

parks.name: Turtle River State Park
parks.description: Wooded riverside park great for hiking and skiing.

motto: Liberty and union, now and forever, one and inseparable

quarter.design: Two grazing bison with sunset over rugged Badlands buttes in the background, showing the state's wide-open prairie.

funFacts: The geographic center of North America is in North Dakota, near the town of Rugby.
North Dakota leads the country in growing sunflowers, flaxseed, and honey.
The International Peace Garden on the border with Canada celebrates the long friendship between the two nations.

flag.description: A dark blue field with a bald eagle clutching arrows and an olive branch, modeled on the banner carried by North Dakota troops in the Spanish-American War and Philippine-American War.

name: Ohio

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Erie
physicalFeatures.description: The shallowest of the Great Lakes laps Ohio's entire northern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its shallow water freezes over completely in cold winters.

physicalFeatures.name: Ohio River
physicalFeatures.description: The Ohio River forms the state's entire southern boundary.
physicalFeatures.fact: Pioneers floated flatboats down it to settle the American Midwest.

physicalFeatures.name: Allegheny Plateau
physicalFeatures.description: A high, hilly region in eastern Ohio, deeply cut by valleys.
physicalFeatures.fact: Glaciers stopped right at its western edge, leaving the hills untouched.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Flat, rich farmland covers western Ohio — perfect for corn and soybeans.
physicalFeatures.fact: Ohio's flat west was scraped smooth by Ice Age glaciers.

physicalFeatures.name: Campbell Hill
physicalFeatures.description: Ohio's highest point — a quiet rise at a vocational school in the middle of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,549 feet, it's marked by a sign in a school parking lot.

physicalFeatures.name: Cuyahoga River
physicalFeatures.description: A winding river that flows through Cleveland to Lake Erie.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'crooked river' in the Iroquoian language.

parks.name: Cuyahoga Valley National Park
parks.description: A green river valley between Cleveland and Akron, with waterfalls and a train.

parks.name: Hopewell Culture National Historical Park
parks.description: Mysterious ancient earthen mounds built 2,000 years ago.

parks.name: Hocking Hills State Park
parks.description: Sandstone cliffs, deep gorges, and waterfalls in southern Ohio.

parks.name: Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial
parks.description: A 352-foot column honoring a key naval battle on Lake Erie.

parks.name: Dayton Aviation Heritage National Historical Park
parks.description: The hometown sites of the Wright brothers, inventors of the airplane.

parks.name: James A. Garfield National Historic Site
parks.description: Home of America's 20th president.

parks.name: Cedar Point
parks.description: Famous Lake Erie peninsula with roller coasters and beaches.

parks.name: Mohican State Park
parks.description: Forested gorge popular for canoeing.

parks.name: John Bryan State Park
parks.description: Beautiful Little Miami River gorge.

parks.name: Lake Erie Bluffs
parks.description: Towering bluffs along Lake Erie's shore.

motto: With God, all things are possible

quarter.design: An early airplane and an astronaut, honoring Ohio's role as the 'Birthplace of Aviation Pioneers' — home of the Wright Brothers and astronauts John Glenn and Neil Armstrong.

funFacts: Ohio is the birthplace of seven U.S. Presidents — more than any state except Virginia.
Neil Armstrong, the first person to walk on the moon, was born in Wapakoneta, Ohio.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame is in Cleveland, Ohio, the city where the phrase 'rock and roll' was first popularized.

flag.description: A unique five-sided 'swallow-tailed' burgee with red and white stripes and a blue triangle bearing 17 stars and a white-bordered red disk—the only non-rectangular U.S. state flag.

name: Oklahoma

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Open prairies and ranchland cover most of Oklahoma.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Oklahoma plains were once part of America's great Dust Bowl in the 1930s.

physicalFeatures.name: Red River
physicalFeatures.description: A reddish river that forms the entire border between Oklahoma and Texas.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its red color comes from clay washed out of the surrounding hills.

physicalFeatures.name: Wichita Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Old, rounded granite mountains rising suddenly from the southwest plains.
physicalFeatures.fact: They're more than 500 million years old — among the oldest in the U.S.

physicalFeatures.name: Arkansas River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that cuts across Oklahoma on its way to the Mississippi.
physicalFeatures.fact: Tulsa is one of the country's most inland river ports.

physicalFeatures.name: Black Mesa
physicalFeatures.description: Oklahoma's highest point, a flat-topped basalt mesa in the panhandle.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 4,975 feet, it's covered by a black lava flow from a long-extinct volcano.

physicalFeatures.name: Tallgrass Prairie
physicalFeatures.description: A vast remnant of America's once-endless tallgrass prairie.
physicalFeatures.fact: Less than 4% of the original tallgrass prairie still exists anywhere.

parks.name: Chickasaw National Recreation Area
parks.description: Cold mineral springs, swimming holes, and forested hills.

parks.name: Washita Battlefield National Historic Site
parks.description: A site that tells the story of a tragic frontier battle in 1868.

parks.name: Beavers Bend State Park
parks.description: A scenic mountain park with a clear, cold river good for fly fishing.

parks.name: Robbers Cave State Park
parks.description: Sandstone caves and forests where outlaws really did hide in the 1800s.

parks.name: Wichita Mountains Wildlife Refuge
parks.description: Granite peaks and prairies where wild bison and longhorns roam free.

parks.name: Oklahoma City National Memorial
parks.description: Honors the victims of the 1995 bombing.

parks.name: Fort Smith National Historic Site
parks.description: Frontier fort that became the courtroom of 'Hanging Judge' Parker.

parks.name: Quartz Mountain State Park
parks.description: 1.4-billion-year-old granite mountains.

parks.name: Roman Nose State Park
parks.description: Canyons and crystal-clear springs.

parks.name: Black Mesa State Park
parks.description: Sits at Oklahoma's highest point in the panhandle.

parks.name: Lake Murray State Park
parks.description: Oklahoma's largest state park.

quarter.design: A scissor-tailed flycatcher in flight over a field of Indian blanket wildflowers.

funFacts: Oklahoma is home to 39 federally recognized Native American tribes — more than any other state.
The world's first parking meter was installed in Oklahoma City in 1935.
Oklahoma has the largest deposit of pink granite in the world, found in the Wichita Mountains.

flag.description: A sky-blue field bearing an Osage warrior's buffalo-hide shield decorated with eagle feathers, crossed by a peace pipe and an olive branch symbolizing peace between Native peoples and settlers.

name: Oregon

physicalFeatures.name: Cascade Range
physicalFeatures.description: A line of snow-capped volcanic peaks running north-south through Oregon.
physicalFeatures.fact: Most peaks in this range are actually volcanoes.

physicalFeatures.name: Columbia River
physicalFeatures.description: A great river that forms most of Oregon's northern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries more water than any other river in the western U.S.

physicalFeatures.name: Pacific Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A wild, rocky coast with sea stacks, cliffs, and hidden beaches.
physicalFeatures.fact: Oregon's entire coast is public — every inch belongs to the people.

physicalFeatures.name: Crater Lake
physicalFeatures.description: The deepest lake in the United States, sitting inside a collapsed volcano.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's 1,949 feet deep — and its water is bluer than the sky.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Hood
physicalFeatures.description: Oregon's tallest peak, a snow-capped volcano you can see from Portland.
physicalFeatures.fact: Even in August, you can ski on its glaciers.

physicalFeatures.name: Hells Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: The deepest river canyon in North America, on Oregon's eastern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's nearly 8,000 feet deep — even deeper than the Grand Canyon.

parks.name: Crater Lake National Park
parks.description: The deepest lake in America, filling a collapsed volcano with incredibly blue water.

parks.name: Oregon Caves National Monument
parks.description: Marble caves carved by underground rivers in the Siskiyou Mountains.

parks.name: John Day Fossil Beds National Monument
parks.description: Colorful hills full of fossils from animals that lived 45 million years ago.

parks.name: Smith Rock State Park
parks.description: Towering golden cliffs and a winding river — a famous rock-climbing spot.

parks.name: Columbia River Gorge National Scenic Area
parks.description: A river canyon lined with cliffs and dozens of tall waterfalls.

parks.name: Lewis and Clark National Historical Park
parks.description: Where Lewis and Clark wintered after reaching the Pacific.

parks.name: Newberry National Volcanic Monument
parks.description: Massive volcano with obsidian flows and lakes.

parks.name: Silver Falls State Park
parks.description: 'Crown jewel' of Oregon parks with ten waterfalls.

parks.name: Ecola State Park
parks.description: Iconic Pacific Coast views of Haystack Rock.

parks.name: Cape Lookout State Park
parks.description: Forested headland jutting into the Pacific.

parks.name: Oswald West State Park
parks.description: Old-growth coastal rainforest above secluded Short Sand Beach.

quarter.design: Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the U.S., with Wizard Island and surrounding evergreen trees.

funFacts: Crater Lake in Oregon is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,949 feet — formed inside a collapsed volcano.
Oregon has the only U.S. state flag with two different designs on its front and back.
The shortest river in the world, the D River, is in Oregon and is only 120 feet long.

flag.description: A navy-blue field with the state shield, the word 'STATE OF OREGON', and the year 1859 in gold on the front—and a gold beaver on the back, making Oregon the only U.S. state with a two-sided flag.

name: Pennsylvania

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Forested ridges and valleys form long parallel waves across the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: The ridges look like wrinkles when seen from space.

physicalFeatures.name: Allegheny Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: The western part of the Appalachians, full of mountain laurel and black bears.
physicalFeatures.fact: They contain the oldest exposed rock east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: Susquehanna River
physicalFeatures.description: One of the oldest rivers in the world, winding through Pennsylvania to the Chesapeake.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's older than the mountains it crosses — it kept its path while they pushed up around it.

physicalFeatures.name: Ohio River
physicalFeatures.description: The Ohio River begins in Pittsburgh where two rivers come together.
physicalFeatures.fact: You can see all three rivers meet from the top of a hill in downtown Pittsburgh.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Davis
physicalFeatures.description: Pennsylvania's highest point, a quiet hill in the Allegheny Mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 3,213 feet, a stone observation tower stands on top.

physicalFeatures.name: Delaware River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that forms Pennsylvania's entire eastern border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Washington crossed this river on Christmas night to attack at Trenton.

parks.name: Independence National Historical Park
parks.description: Where the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution were signed.

parks.name: Gettysburg National Military Park
parks.description: The Pennsylvania farm fields that hosted the largest battle of the Civil War.

parks.name: Valley Forge National Historical Park
parks.description: Where Washington's army survived the brutal winter of 1777-78.

parks.name: Ohiopyle State Park
parks.description: A wild stretch of the Youghiogheny River — one of the East's best whitewater rivers.

parks.name: Allegheny National Forest
parks.description: Pennsylvania's only national forest, with quiet streams and black bears.

parks.name: Allegheny Portage Railroad National Historic Site
parks.description: Engineering wonder that climbed the Allegheny Mountains.

parks.name: Flight 93 National Memorial
parks.description: Honors the heroes of United Flight 93 on 9/11.

parks.name: Fort Necessity National Battlefield
parks.description: Site of the first battle of the French and Indian War.

parks.name: Ricketts Glen State Park
parks.description: 21 named waterfalls in a glacial gorge.

parks.name: Presque Isle State Park
parks.description: Sandy peninsula jutting into Lake Erie.

parks.name: Cherry Springs State Park
parks.description: One of the darkest skies in the eastern U.S., perfect for stargazing.

quarter.design: A statue called 'Commonwealth', an outline of the state, and a keystone, with the words 'Virtue, Liberty, Independence'.

funFacts: The Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution were both signed in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Hershey, Pennsylvania, is the chocolate capital of the U.S. — even the streetlamps are shaped like Hershey's Kisses.
Pennsylvania is home to the oldest continuously operating drive-in movie theater in the country, opened in 1934.

flag.description: A blue field bearing the state coat of arms: a shield with a ship, plow, and sheaves of wheat, flanked by two black horses and topped by a bald eagle, with an olive branch and cornstalk below.

name: Rhode Island

physicalFeatures.name: Narragansett Bay
physicalFeatures.description: A long bay that cuts deep into Rhode Island, dividing the state in half.
physicalFeatures.fact: The bay covers about 10% of the state's total area.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Over 400 miles of rugged coastline despite being the smallest state.
physicalFeatures.fact: That's more miles of coast per square mile than any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Block Island Sound
physicalFeatures.description: A stretch of ocean between Long Island and Rhode Island's Block Island.
physicalFeatures.fact: Block Island was named after a Dutch explorer in 1614.

physicalFeatures.name: Blackstone River
physicalFeatures.description: A small river where America's industrial revolution began with the first mill.
physicalFeatures.fact: America's first water-powered factory opened along it in 1793.

physicalFeatures.name: Jerimoth Hill
physicalFeatures.description: Rhode Island's highest point — a tiny wooded rise.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 812 feet, it's the lowest 'highest point' of any state in the Northeast.

physicalFeatures.name: Block Island
physicalFeatures.description: A scenic island 12 miles off the coast, reached by ferry.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its cliffs are made of clay deposited by Ice Age glaciers.

parks.name: Roger Williams National Memorial
parks.description: Honors the man who founded Rhode Island as a place of religious freedom.

parks.name: Blackstone River Valley National Historical Park
parks.description: Where the American industrial revolution began in 1793.

parks.name: Colt State Park
parks.description: 464 waterfront acres on Narragansett Bay with bike paths and gardens.

parks.name: Beavertail State Park
parks.description: A rocky point with one of America's oldest working lighthouses.

parks.name: Fort Adams State Park
parks.description: A massive 19th-century stone fort guarding Newport Harbor.

parks.name: Lincoln Woods State Park
parks.description: Wooded park with a pond for swimming.

parks.name: Burlingame State Park
parks.description: Camping park on Watchaug Pond.

parks.name: Goddard Memorial State Park
parks.description: Forested park with a saltwater beach.

quarter.design: A vintage sailboat racing across Narragansett Bay, with the Pell Bridge in the background and the words 'The Ocean State'.

funFacts: Rhode Island is the smallest U.S. state — only 48 miles long and 37 miles wide.
Despite the name, Rhode Island is not an island — but the state does include more than 30 islands.
Rhode Island was the first of the 13 colonies to declare independence from Britain, on May 4, 1776.

flag.description: A white field with a gold anchor in the center surrounded by 13 gold stars, with a blue ribbon below bearing the single-word state motto 'Hope'.

name: South Carolina

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: 187 miles of warm beaches, sea islands, and salt marsh.
physicalFeatures.fact: South Carolina's coast has more sea islands than any other state.

physicalFeatures.name: Blue Ridge Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A small slice of the Blue Ridge reaches into South Carolina's upstate corner.
physicalFeatures.fact: From their peaks you can see Georgia, North Carolina, and Tennessee.

physicalFeatures.name: Savannah River
physicalFeatures.description: A wide river that forms South Carolina's entire western border with Georgia.
physicalFeatures.fact: Big ocean ships can travel up it all the way to Augusta.

physicalFeatures.name: Congaree Swamp
physicalFeatures.description: An old-growth bottomland forest with some of the tallest hardwood trees in America.
physicalFeatures.fact: It contains the largest old-growth bottomland hardwood forest in the U.S.

physicalFeatures.name: Sassafras Mountain
physicalFeatures.description: South Carolina's highest point, with a new observation tower at the top.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 3,553 feet, you can stand on the South Carolina-North Carolina border up there.

physicalFeatures.name: Sea Islands
physicalFeatures.description: A chain of low, marshy barrier islands along South Carolina's coast.
physicalFeatures.fact: Sweet grass baskets, woven on these islands, are a 300-year-old tradition.

parks.name: Congaree National Park
parks.description: Towering bottomland trees you can explore on a wooden boardwalk.

parks.name: Fort Sumter National Monument
parks.description: The island fort where the first shots of the Civil War were fired.

parks.name: Cowpens National Battlefield
parks.description: Site of a key American victory in the Revolutionary War.

parks.name: Hunting Island State Park
parks.description: South Carolina's most-visited state park, with a climbable old lighthouse.

parks.name: Caesars Head State Park
parks.description: A mountain park with a sheer cliff overlooking the Blue Ridge Mountains.

parks.name: Fort Sumter and Fort Moultrie National Historical Park
parks.description: Where the Civil War began.

parks.name: Kings Mountain National Military Park
parks.description: Site of a turning-point Revolutionary War battle.

parks.name: Charles Pinckney National Historic Site
parks.description: Home of a signer of the Constitution.

parks.name: Myrtle Beach State Park
parks.description: Maritime forest right on the Atlantic Ocean.

parks.name: Table Rock State Park
parks.description: Named for the granite dome that looms over Pinnacle Lake.

parks.name: Edisto Beach State Park
parks.description: Sea Island beach famous for shark teeth.

quarter.design: The state outline with a Carolina wren, a sabal palmetto tree, and a yellow jessamine flower — all state symbols.

funFacts: Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor is where the first shots of the Civil War were fired in 1861.
South Carolina grows more peaches than Georgia, even though Georgia is called the Peach State.
The only commercial tea plantation in the United States is on Wadmalaw Island, South Carolina.

flag.description: An indigo-blue field with a white crescent in the upper-left corner and a white palmetto tree at the center, honoring the palmetto-log fort that repelled a British attack in 1776.

name: South Dakota

physicalFeatures.name: Black Hills
physicalFeatures.description: Dark, pine-covered mountains rising from the prairie — sacred to Native peoples.
physicalFeatures.fact: They look black from a distance because of their dense, dark forests.

physicalFeatures.name: Badlands
physicalFeatures.description: Sharp, striped buttes and gullies carved out of soft prairie rock.
physicalFeatures.fact: They contain one of the world's richest beds of fossil mammals.

physicalFeatures.name: Missouri River
physicalFeatures.description: The Missouri splits South Dakota into two very different landscapes.
physicalFeatures.fact: East of the river is farmland — west of it, ranchland and badlands.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Wide, rolling grasslands cover most of South Dakota.
physicalFeatures.fact: The state has more cows than people.

physicalFeatures.name: Black Elk Peak
physicalFeatures.description: The highest peak east of the Rockies, deep in the Black Hills.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 7,242 feet, its summit has a stone fire-lookout tower.

physicalFeatures.name: Spearfish Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: A deep limestone canyon with waterfalls and golden cliffs.
physicalFeatures.fact: Scenes from a famous Western movie were filmed here.

parks.name: Badlands National Park
parks.description: Wild striped buttes, gullies, and rolling prairie full of bison.

parks.name: Wind Cave National Park
parks.description: One of the world's longest caves, with rare 'boxwork' rock formations.

parks.name: Mount Rushmore National Memorial
parks.description: Four 60-foot-tall presidents carved into the side of a mountain.

parks.name: Custer State Park
parks.description: A huge park with herds of free-roaming bison, burros, and pronghorn.

parks.name: Jewel Cave National Monument
parks.description: The third-longest cave in the world, full of sparkling crystals.

parks.name: Minuteman Missile National Historic Site
parks.description: Cold War nuclear missile silo and control center.

parks.name: Bear Butte State Park
parks.description: Sacred mountain to many Plains tribes.

parks.name: Newton Hills State Park
parks.description: Wooded ridge in southeastern South Dakota.

parks.name: Palisades State Park
parks.description: Sioux quartzite cliffs along Split Rock Creek.

parks.name: Lewis and Clark Recreation Area
parks.description: Big reservoir park on the Missouri River.

parks.name: Roughlock Falls
parks.description: Beautiful waterfall in Spearfish Canyon.

quarter.design: Mount Rushmore featuring the four presidents' faces, a Chinese ring-necked pheasant in the foreground, and wheat ears framing the design.

funFacts: Mount Rushmore, with the carved faces of four U.S. Presidents, took 14 years to finish and stands 60 feet tall.
The Badlands of South Dakota contain one of the richest fossil beds in the world.
South Dakota has more shoreline miles of lakes than the entire state of Florida.

flag.description: A sky-blue field with the state seal in gold at the center, surrounded by golden sun rays and the words 'SOUTH DAKOTA' and 'THE MOUNT RUSHMORE STATE'.

name: Tennessee

physicalFeatures.name: Great Smoky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Misty blue mountains shared with North Carolina, full of wildflowers and bears.
physicalFeatures.fact: They're called 'smoky' for the natural blue fog that rises from the trees.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The Mississippi forms Tennessee's entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Memphis grew up on its banks as the busiest cotton-shipping city in America.

physicalFeatures.name: Tennessee River
physicalFeatures.description: A winding river that curves through the state in a giant U-shape.
physicalFeatures.fact: It actually leaves Tennessee, dips into Alabama, and comes back in.

physicalFeatures.name: Cumberland Plateau
physicalFeatures.description: A high, flat-topped land of forests, gorges, and waterfalls in the middle of the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's one of the longest hardwood-forested plateaus in the world.

physicalFeatures.name: Clingmans Dome
physicalFeatures.description: Tennessee's highest mountain, with a futuristic spiral observation tower.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 6,643 feet, it's the highest point on the entire Appalachian Trail.

physicalFeatures.name: Reelfoot Lake
physicalFeatures.description: A swampy lake that was formed by a series of giant earthquakes in 1812.
physicalFeatures.fact: The quakes were so strong they made the Mississippi flow backwards briefly.

parks.name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
parks.description: America's most-visited national park, with misty peaks and waterfalls.

parks.name: Big South Fork National River and Recreation Area
parks.description: Cumberland Plateau wilderness with deep gorges and natural arches.

parks.name: Shiloh National Military Park
parks.description: Site of one of the largest battles of the early Civil War.

parks.name: Fall Creek Falls State Park
parks.description: Home to one of the highest waterfalls in the eastern U.S. at 256 feet.

parks.name: Cherokee National Forest
parks.description: Tennessee's only national forest, covering the eastern mountains.

parks.name: Big South Fork National River
parks.description: Wild river canyon with natural arches.

parks.name: Andrew Johnson National Historic Site
parks.description: Home and tailor shop of America's 17th president.

parks.name: Manhattan Project National Historical Park
parks.description: Oak Ridge site that helped build the atomic bomb.

parks.name: Cumberland Mountain State Park
parks.description: CCC-built park with the largest masonry dam they made.

parks.name: Roan Mountain State Park
parks.description: Famous for natural rhododendron gardens at 6,000 feet.

parks.name: Reelfoot Lake State Park
parks.description: Lake formed by an 1811 earthquake, full of cypress trees.

parks.name: Burgess Falls State Park
parks.description: Four waterfalls in less than a mile.

quarter.design: Three musical instruments — a fiddle, a trumpet, and a guitar — and a banner reading 'Musical Heritage', honoring Tennessee's three musical regions.

funFacts: Tennessee borders eight other states, tied with Missouri for the most state neighbors.
Nashville is called 'Music City' and is the home of country music and the Grand Ole Opry.
Great Smoky Mountains National Park, partly in Tennessee, is the most visited national park in the U.S.

flag.description: A red field with a blue circle bearing three white stars (representing Tennessee's three Grand Divisions—East, Middle, and West), bordered in white, with a blue vertical bar on the fly side.

name: Texas

physicalFeatures.name: Rio Grande
physicalFeatures.description: The river that forms the entire border between Texas and Mexico.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name means 'big river' in Spanish.

physicalFeatures.name: Gulf of Mexico Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Over 360 miles of warm beaches, bays, and barrier islands.
physicalFeatures.fact: Padre Island is the world's longest stretch of undeveloped barrier island.

physicalFeatures.name: Chihuahuan Desert
physicalFeatures.description: A high, rocky desert covering all of West Texas.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the largest desert in North America.

physicalFeatures.name: Edwards Plateau
physicalFeatures.description: Limestone hill country full of springs, caves, and live oaks.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its caves are home to millions of bats that fly out together at sunset.

physicalFeatures.name: Guadalupe Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A jagged mountain range hiding the state's highest peak.
physicalFeatures.fact: They were formed from a giant fossilized coral reef.

physicalFeatures.name: Guadalupe Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Texas's highest point, the tip of an ancient buried reef.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 8,751 feet, it overlooks hundreds of miles of desert.

physicalFeatures.name: Palo Duro Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: The second-largest canyon in the United States, often called the 'Grand Canyon of Texas.'
physicalFeatures.fact: It's 120 miles long and up to 800 feet deep.

parks.name: Big Bend National Park
parks.description: A vast desert wilderness where the Rio Grande makes a giant U-turn.

parks.name: Guadalupe Mountains National Park
parks.description: Texas's tallest mountains, hidden in a remote desert range.

parks.name: Padre Island National Seashore
parks.description: The world's longest undeveloped barrier island.

parks.name: Palo Duro Canyon State Park
parks.description: The 'Grand Canyon of Texas,' with red and orange cliffs.

parks.name: Big Thicket National Preserve
parks.description: A swampy, jungle-like forest where Eastern and Western U.S. habitats blend.

parks.name: San Antonio Missions National Historical Park
parks.description: Four Spanish missions including the famous Alamo.

parks.name: Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park
parks.description: Boyhood home and 'Texas White House' of the 36th president.

parks.name: Waco Mammoth National Monument
parks.description: Fossil bed of an Ice Age mammoth herd.

parks.name: Enchanted Rock State Natural Area
parks.description: Massive pink granite dome you can hike to the top of.

parks.name: Garner State Park
parks.description: Texas's most popular state park on the Frio River.

parks.name: Caddo Lake State Park
parks.description: Mysterious cypress swamp on the Texas-Louisiana border.

quarter.design: An outline of the state with a single bright star to the side, and a lariat encircling the design with the words 'The Lone Star State'.

funFacts: Texas is the second-largest U.S. state, big enough that El Paso is closer to California than to Houston.
Texas was its own independent country, the Republic of Texas, from 1836 to 1845 before joining the U.S.
Texas has more cattle and more farms than any other U.S. state.

flag.description: A vertical blue stripe with a single large white five-pointed star on the hoist side, beside horizontal stripes of white over red—nicknamed the 'Lone Star Flag'.

name: Utah

physicalFeatures.name: Great Salt Lake
physicalFeatures.description: The largest salt lake in the Western Hemisphere — saltier than the ocean.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so salty you can float on top of it without trying.

physicalFeatures.name: Wasatch Range
physicalFeatures.description: A long mountain range running north-south, with Salt Lake City right at its base.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some of the world's best powder snow falls on these mountains.

physicalFeatures.name: Colorado River
physicalFeatures.description: The Colorado carves through Utah's red rock country before reaching Arizona.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's the river that made the Grand Canyon.

physicalFeatures.name: Bonneville Salt Flats
physicalFeatures.description: A bone-white salt plain so flat that cars set land-speed records on it.
physicalFeatures.fact: After it rains, it turns into a giant natural mirror.

physicalFeatures.name: Kings Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Utah's highest mountain, deep in the rugged Uinta Range.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 13,528 feet, it's a long backpacking trip just to reach the base.

physicalFeatures.name: Zion Canyon
physicalFeatures.description: A deep canyon of red and orange sandstone cliffs cut by the Virgin River.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its walls rise more than 2,000 feet straight up from the canyon floor.

parks.name: Zion National Park
parks.description: Towering red and orange sandstone cliffs cut by the Virgin River.

parks.name: Bryce Canyon National Park
parks.description: Thousands of red rock spires called hoodoos in a giant natural amphitheater.

parks.name: Arches National Park
parks.description: Home to over 2,000 natural stone arches sculpted by wind and weather.

parks.name: Antelope Island State Park
parks.description: A large island in the Great Salt Lake with free-roaming bison and antelope.

parks.name: Canyonlands National Park
parks.description: A wild land of canyons, mesas, and buttes carved by two rivers.

parks.name: Capitol Reef National Park
parks.description: A 100-mile-long wrinkle in the Earth's crust full of cliffs and hidden orchards.

parks.name: Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument
parks.description: Vast wilderness of cliffs, slot canyons, and dinosaur fossils.

parks.name: Cedar Breaks National Monument
parks.description: Half-mile-deep natural amphitheater of red and orange rock.

parks.name: Natural Bridges National Monument
parks.description: Three of the world's largest natural stone bridges.

parks.name: Dinosaur National Monument
parks.description: Quarry wall studded with real dinosaur bones.

parks.name: Goblin Valley State Park
parks.description: Field of mushroom-shaped 'goblin' rocks kids can run through.

parks.name: Dead Horse Point State Park
parks.description: Dramatic overlook 2,000 feet above the Colorado River.

parks.name: Snow Canyon State Park
parks.description: Red and white sandstone canyon near St. George.

parks.name: Coral Pink Sand Dunes State Park
parks.description: Salmon-colored dunes you can sandboard down.

parks.name: Sand Hollow State Park
parks.description: Bright blue reservoir surrounded by red sandstone.

quarter.design: An old steam locomotive and a modern train meeting at Promontory Point, marking the completion of the transcontinental railroad in 1869.

funFacts: Utah is the only state where every county contains some part of a national forest.
The Great Salt Lake is the largest salt lake in the Western Hemisphere.
Utah has five national parks: Arches, Bryce Canyon, Canyonlands, Capitol Reef, and Zion.

flag.description: A modern flag with a navy-blue 'mountain' base and white sky, centered with a gold beehive on a hexagon, an eight-pointed star above, and red side stripes representing Utah's red-rock heritage.

name: Vermont

physicalFeatures.name: Green Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A long range running the full length of Vermont, blazing with fall colors.
physicalFeatures.fact: The state's name comes from the French words for 'green mountain.'

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Champlain
physicalFeatures.description: A long, narrow lake forming much of Vermont's western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some people say a creature named Champ lives in its waters.

physicalFeatures.name: Connecticut River
physicalFeatures.description: The Connecticut forms Vermont's entire eastern border with New Hampshire.
physicalFeatures.fact: Even though it's a border, the whole river belongs to New Hampshire.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Mansfield
physicalFeatures.description: Vermont's tallest mountain, with a ridge that looks like a face in profile.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 4,395 feet, its summit ridge looks like a giant lying down.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Willoughby
physicalFeatures.description: A deep glacial lake flanked by sheer mountain cliffs.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's often called New England's most beautiful lake.

physicalFeatures.name: Camel's Hump
physicalFeatures.description: A distinctive mountain whose shape really does look like a camel's back.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's one of only a few Vermont peaks left in totally wild condition.

parks.name: Marsh-Billings-Rockefeller National Historical Park
parks.description: Vermont's only national park, celebrating conservation history.

parks.name: Green Mountain National Forest
parks.description: Vermont's only national forest, covering 400,000 acres of mountains.

parks.name: Mount Mansfield State Forest
parks.description: Surrounding Vermont's tallest peak, with hiking, skiing, and waterfalls.

parks.name: Camel's Hump State Park
parks.description: A wild, undeveloped park around one of Vermont's most beautiful mountains.

parks.name: Smugglers' Notch State Park
parks.description: A narrow mountain pass with giant boulders and a road that closes in winter.

parks.name: Appalachian National Scenic Trail
parks.description: Section of the 2,200-mile trail running through Vermont.

parks.name: Quechee State Park
parks.description: Vermont's 'Little Grand Canyon', 165 feet deep.

parks.name: Mt. Philo State Park
parks.description: Vermont's oldest state park, with Lake Champlain views.

parks.name: Button Bay State Park
parks.description: Lake Champlain park named for round stone 'buttons'.

parks.name: Grand Isle State Park
parks.description: Island park in the middle of Lake Champlain.

parks.name: Little River State Park
parks.description: Camping park near a flooded 1800s farming community.

quarter.design: A man tapping a maple tree to collect sap, with Camel's Hump mountain in the background and the words 'Freedom and Unity'.

funFacts: Vermont produces more maple syrup than any other state — about 2 million gallons a year.
Vermont was an independent republic for 14 years before joining the U.S. in 1791.
About 78 percent of Vermont is covered in forest — one of the most forested states in the country.

flag.description: A blue field with the state coat of arms at the center: a tall pine tree, a cow and sheaves of wheat representing agriculture, and mountains in the background with the word 'Vermont' below.

name: Virginia

physicalFeatures.name: Blue Ridge Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Misty blue mountains running along Virginia's western side.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Skyline Drive runs 105 miles along their crest.

physicalFeatures.name: Chesapeake Bay
physicalFeatures.description: America's largest bay laps Virginia's eastern coast, famous for crabs.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's so big it has its own islands with their own small towns.

physicalFeatures.name: Shenandoah Valley
physicalFeatures.description: A long, fertile valley running between the Blue Ridge and Allegheny mountains.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its name is said to mean 'daughter of the stars' in a Native language.

physicalFeatures.name: Atlantic Coast
physicalFeatures.description: Virginia's coast includes the long sandy Eastern Shore peninsula.
physicalFeatures.fact: The Chesapeake Bay Bridge-Tunnel — 23 miles long — connects the shore to the mainland.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Rogers
physicalFeatures.description: Virginia's highest mountain, with wild ponies grazing on its open balds.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 5,729 feet, its top is covered in spruce-fir forest like northern Maine.

physicalFeatures.name: James River
physicalFeatures.description: Virginia's longest river, flowing past Richmond and into Chesapeake Bay.
physicalFeatures.fact: English settlers founded Jamestown on its banks in 1607.

parks.name: Shenandoah National Park
parks.description: A long mountain park with the famous Skyline Drive along its crest.

parks.name: Colonial National Historical Park
parks.description: Includes Jamestown and Yorktown — where America began and won its independence.

parks.name: Assateague Island National Seashore
parks.description: Wild ponies roam free on this barrier island shared with Maryland.

parks.name: First Landing State Park
parks.description: Where the first English colonists landed in 1607.

parks.name: Great Falls Park
parks.description: Where the Potomac River roars over a series of rocky drops near D.C.

parks.name: Manassas National Battlefield Park
parks.description: Site of two major Civil War battles.

parks.name: Appomattox Court House National Historical Park
parks.description: Where the Civil War ended.

parks.name: Arlington House
parks.description: Robert E. Lee's mansion above Arlington National Cemetery.

parks.name: Booker T. Washington National Monument
parks.description: Birthplace of the famous African American educator.

parks.name: Grayson Highlands State Park
parks.description: Home to wild ponies in the Virginia highlands.

parks.name: Natural Bridge State Park
parks.description: 215-foot natural limestone arch once owned by Thomas Jefferson.

quarter.design: The three ships of Jamestown — the Susan Constant, Godspeed, and Discovery — that brought the first English colonists in 1607, with the words 'Jamestown 1607-2007 Quadricentennial'.

funFacts: Eight U.S. Presidents were born in Virginia — more than any other state.
Jamestown, founded in 1607, was the first permanent English settlement in North America.
The Pentagon, the headquarters of the U.S. Department of Defense, is in Arlington, Virginia and is one of the world's largest office buildings.

flag.description: A deep-blue field with the state seal at the center, depicting the Roman goddess Virtus standing victorious over a defeated tyrant, with the state motto 'Sic Semper Tyrannis' (Thus Always to Tyrants).

name: Washington

physicalFeatures.name: Cascade Range
physicalFeatures.description: Snowy volcanic mountains that split Washington into a wet west and dry east.
physicalFeatures.fact: The range includes several active volcanoes.

physicalFeatures.name: Puget Sound
physicalFeatures.description: A maze of saltwater inlets and islands stretching deep into western Washington.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has more shoreline than the entire coast of California.

physicalFeatures.name: Columbia River
physicalFeatures.description: A massive river that flows through Washington and forms the Oregon border.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries more water than any other river on the West Coast.

physicalFeatures.name: Olympic Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: A rugged range with the rainiest spots in the lower 48 states.
physicalFeatures.fact: Some places get 12 to 14 feet of rain per year.

physicalFeatures.name: Mount Rainier
physicalFeatures.description: A massive snow-capped volcano towering over Seattle.
physicalFeatures.fact: It carries the most glacier ice of any mountain in the lower 48.

physicalFeatures.name: Pacific Coast
physicalFeatures.description: A wild, rocky coast with rainforests reaching right to the beach.
physicalFeatures.fact: Driftwood logs the size of cars wash up on its beaches.

parks.name: Olympic National Park
parks.description: Rainforests, snowy peaks, and wild Pacific coast all in one park.

parks.name: Mount Rainier National Park
parks.description: Towering snow-capped volcano surrounded by wildflower meadows.

parks.name: North Cascades National Park
parks.description: More than 300 glaciers tucked into jagged, rugged peaks.

parks.name: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument
parks.description: A volcano that blew its top off in a famous 1980 eruption.

parks.name: San Juan Islands National Historical Park
parks.description: Islands in Puget Sound where the U.S. and Britain almost went to war over a pig.

parks.name: San Juan Island National Historical Park
parks.description: Site of a bloodless border dispute called the 'Pig War'.

parks.name: Whitman Mission National Historic Site
parks.description: Early Oregon Trail mission site.

parks.name: Deception Pass State Park
parks.description: Washington's most-visited state park, with a dramatic bridge.

parks.name: Cape Disappointment State Park
parks.description: Where Lewis and Clark reached the Pacific.

parks.name: Mount Spokane State Park
parks.description: Washington's largest state park, with a summit lodge.

parks.name: Palouse Falls State Park
parks.description: Washington's official state waterfall, 200 feet tall.

quarter.design: A king salmon leaping in front of Mount Rainier, with the words 'The Evergreen State'.

funFacts: Washington is the only state named after a U.S. President — George Washington.
Mount Rainier is the most glaciated peak in the contiguous U.S., with 25 named glaciers.
Washington grows more apples than any other state — about two-thirds of all the apples eaten in the U.S.

flag.description: A dark-green field (the only U.S. state flag with a green background) bearing a portrait of George Washington at the center, encircled by the words 'The Seal of the State of Washington 1889'.

name: West Virginia

physicalFeatures.name: Appalachian Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Old, forested mountains cover nearly all of West Virginia.
physicalFeatures.fact: West Virginia is the only state entirely inside the Appalachian Mountain region.

physicalFeatures.name: Allegheny Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: The western part of the Appalachians, full of clear streams and steep ridges.
physicalFeatures.fact: They contain some of the most rugged country east of the Mississippi.

physicalFeatures.name: New River Gorge
physicalFeatures.description: A deep canyon cut by one of the oldest rivers in the world.
physicalFeatures.fact: Its bridge is one of the highest vehicle bridges in the U.S.

physicalFeatures.name: Ohio River
physicalFeatures.description: The Ohio River forms West Virginia's entire western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Big coal barges still travel up and down it today.

physicalFeatures.name: Spruce Knob
physicalFeatures.description: West Virginia's tallest mountain, with a spruce forest like northern Canada.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 4,863 feet, it has its own little sub-arctic climate on top.

physicalFeatures.name: Monongahela National Forest
physicalFeatures.description: A vast highland forest of red spruce, hardwoods, and rocky overlooks.
physicalFeatures.fact: It contains some of the highest land east of the Mississippi.

parks.name: New River Gorge National Park
parks.description: A deep, rugged river canyon — America's newest national park.

parks.name: Harpers Ferry National Historical Park
parks.description: A historic town where three states meet at the joining of two rivers.

parks.name: Monongahela National Forest
