parks.description: Almost a million acres of mountains, bogs, caves, and rocky outcrops.

parks.name: Blackwater Falls State Park
parks.description: A 57-foot waterfall whose amber water comes from tannin in spruce trees.

parks.name: Seneca Rocks
parks.description: A massive 900-foot rock fin popular with climbers and hikers.

parks.name: Bluestone National Scenic River
parks.description: Wild river through a remote Appalachian gorge.

parks.name: Gauley River National Recreation Area
parks.description: World-class whitewater river.

parks.name: Babcock State Park
parks.description: Home to the much-photographed Glade Creek Grist Mill.

parks.name: Hawks Nest State Park
parks.description: Overlook 750 feet above the New River with a tramway.

parks.name: Cass Scenic Railroad State Park
parks.description: Ride a restored logging steam train up Bald Knob.

parks.name: Pipestem Resort State Park
parks.description: Includes an aerial tramway down to the Bluestone Gorge.

quarter.design: The New River Gorge Bridge, one of the longest single-arch bridges in the world, spanning a deep mountain canyon.

funFacts: West Virginia is the only state created by separating from another state — it split from Virginia during the Civil War in 1863.
The state's average elevation is 1,500 feet — the highest of any state east of the Mississippi River.
The New River in West Virginia is one of the oldest rivers in the world, despite its name.

flag.description: A white field bordered in blue containing the state coat of arms: a farmer and a miner flanking a boulder marked '1863' (the year of statehood), with rifles and a Phrygian cap below symbolizing the fight for liberty.

name: Wisconsin

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Superior
physicalFeatures.description: The world's largest freshwater lake by area touches Wisconsin's far north.
physicalFeatures.fact: It holds more water than the other four Great Lakes combined.

physicalFeatures.name: Lake Michigan
physicalFeatures.description: Forms Wisconsin's entire eastern border, with cliffs and beaches.
physicalFeatures.fact: Milwaukee and Green Bay both sit on Wisconsin's Lake Michigan shore.

physicalFeatures.name: Mississippi River
physicalFeatures.description: The Mississippi forms much of Wisconsin's western border.
physicalFeatures.fact: Tall bluffs rise more than 500 feet above the river along this stretch.

physicalFeatures.name: Wisconsin River
physicalFeatures.description: Wisconsin's longest river, flowing 430 miles across the state.
physicalFeatures.fact: It's known for the strange sandstone cliffs called the Wisconsin Dells.

physicalFeatures.name: Door Peninsula
physicalFeatures.description: A long peninsula sticking out into Lake Michigan, like Wisconsin's thumb.
physicalFeatures.fact: It has more lighthouses than any other county in the U.S.

physicalFeatures.name: Timms Hill
physicalFeatures.description: Wisconsin's highest point, a quiet wooded hill with an observation tower.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 1,951 feet, you can see for miles in every direction from the top.

parks.name: Apostle Islands National Lakeshore
parks.description: A scattering of 21 wild islands and sandstone cliffs in Lake Superior.

parks.name: Saint Croix National Scenic Riverway
parks.description: A wild, clear river protected for canoeing and tubing.

parks.name: Devil's Lake State Park
parks.description: A deep lake ringed by 500-foot quartzite cliffs.

parks.name: Peninsula State Park
parks.description: Wisconsin's most-visited state park, on a wooded Lake Michigan peninsula.

parks.name: Ice Age National Scenic Trail
parks.description: A 1,200-mile trail tracing the southern edge of the last Ice Age glaciers.

parks.name: Wyalusing State Park
parks.description: Where the Wisconsin and Mississippi rivers meet.

parks.name: Interstate State Park
parks.description: Wisconsin's oldest state park, with deep glacial potholes.

parks.name: Pattison State Park
parks.description: Home to Big Manitou Falls, Wisconsin's tallest waterfall.

parks.name: Copper Falls State Park
parks.description: Series of waterfalls cutting through ancient lava.

parks.name: Willow River State Park
parks.description: Beautiful Willow Falls and a swimming pond.

quarter.design: A cow, a round of cheese, and an ear of corn, with the words 'Forward', representing Wisconsin's dairy and farming traditions.

funFacts: Wisconsin produces more cheese than any other state — about 3.4 billion pounds a year.
Wisconsin has 15,074 lakes — more than 6 lakes per square mile in some areas.
The ice cream sundae was invented in Two Rivers, Wisconsin in 1881.

flag.description: A dark blue field bearing the state coat of arms with a sailor and a miner flanking a shield, with the word 'WISCONSIN' above and '1848' (the year of statehood) below.

name: Wyoming

physicalFeatures.name: Rocky Mountains
physicalFeatures.description: Wyoming is mostly mountains — the Rockies run through it in several ranges.
physicalFeatures.fact: Wyoming has more land above 6,000 feet than any other state outside Alaska.

physicalFeatures.name: Yellowstone River
physicalFeatures.description: The longest free-flowing river in the lower 48 states starts in Wyoming.
physicalFeatures.fact: It flows 692 miles without a single dam blocking it.

physicalFeatures.name: Grand Teton
physicalFeatures.description: A sharp, snow-capped granite peak that rises straight from a flat valley floor.
physicalFeatures.fact: It rises 7,000 feet above the valley with no foothills in front.

physicalFeatures.name: Great Plains
physicalFeatures.description: Wide, windswept grasslands cover Wyoming's eastern third.
physicalFeatures.fact: Wyoming has fewer people than any other U.S. state.

physicalFeatures.name: Gannett Peak
physicalFeatures.description: Wyoming's tallest mountain, hidden in the rugged Wind River Range.
physicalFeatures.fact: At 13,809 feet, it takes a multi-day backpacking trip just to climb it.

physicalFeatures.name: Devils Tower
physicalFeatures.description: A flat-topped tower of fluted volcanic rock rising 867 feet above the plains.
physicalFeatures.fact: It was America's first national monument.

parks.name: Yellowstone National Park
parks.description: America's first national park, with geysers, hot springs, and herds of bison.

parks.name: Grand Teton National Park
parks.description: A row of jagged peaks rising sharply from a valley of lakes and rivers.

parks.name: Devils Tower National Monument
parks.description: America's first national monument, a striking flat-topped rock tower.

parks.name: Fossil Butte National Monument
parks.description: A flat-topped hill packed with fossils from a 50-million-year-old lake.

parks.name: Bighorn National Forest
parks.description: A high mountain forest with alpine meadows and ancient stone rings.

parks.name: Fort Laramie National Historic Site
parks.description: Frontier fort that protected the Oregon Trail.

parks.name: Bighorn Canyon National Recreation Area
parks.description: 1,000-foot-deep canyon with wild horses.

parks.name: Sinks Canyon State Park
parks.description: River that flows into a cave and reappears a quarter-mile away.

parks.name: Curt Gowdy State Park
parks.description: Three lakes and giant granite boulders.

parks.name: Hot Springs State Park
parks.description: One of the world's largest mineral hot springs.

parks.name: Boysen State Park
parks.description: Big reservoir park at the entrance to Wind River Canyon.

parks.name: Buffalo Bill State Park
parks.description: Park around Buffalo Bill Dam near Cody.

quarter.design: A bucking horse with a rider, the same image used on Wyoming license plates, with the words 'The Equality State'.

funFacts: Wyoming is the least populated U.S. state — fewer people live there than in many single cities.
Yellowstone, mostly in Wyoming, was the world's first national park, established in 1872.
Wyoming was the first state in the U.S. to give women the right to vote, in 1869.

flag.description: A blue field with a white border and red outer border, centered with the silhouette of a bison branded with the state seal—designed by Verna Keays and adopted after a 1916 contest.
